Deniz is developing a modular and gamified robotic system to enhance engagement in stroke patients with upper limb impairments as part of her PhD research. Her studies include creating a cost-effective robotic system for independent use, evaluating adaptive gamified exercises, and building a community-based social platform for virtual rehab sessions to study its effects on engagement. This work aims to provide remote, personalized rehabilitation that is both accessible and engaging.

Deniz is a PhD student in Biomedical Engineering at the University of Toronto, specializing in Robotics under Dr. Alex Mihailidis. Her academic journey includes a BASc in Engineering Science (Aerospace), an MHSc in Clinical Engineering, and an MSc in Rehabilitation Sciences. She is dedicated to developing affordable, practical healthcare solutions that significantly improve the quality of life.

I am foodie, I love outdoor activities, I love hiking and doing triathlon races.

Experience University of Toronto 6 years Doctoral Student September 2022 - Present  (3 years) Toronto, Ontario, Canada My current work involves developing a modular, gamified robotic rehabilitation system to improve engagement and motivation in stroke rehabilitation for upper limb impairments. The Orthopaedic Biomechanics Laboratory Graduate Student September 2016 - August 2019  (3 years)   Page 1 of 5     Sunnybrook Research Institute My graduate MHSc thesis focused on developing and validating a navigation workflow for the vertebral radio-frequency ablation (RFA) of spinal metastases. This project required an integration of wide range of technologies such as cone beam CT scanner, optical tracking camera and markers, RFA system, and software platforms to track and visualize the RFA procedure. The final developed navigation workflow was validated in animal studies.

My research introduced me to the challenges faced while developing biomedical technologies intended to be biocompatible and safe for human use, which I believe is paramount to producing clinical focused and ground breaking medical devices.

• • • •Research Article 3D Video Tracking Technology in the Assessment of Orofacial Impairments in Neurological Disease: Clinical Validation Deniz Jafari,a,b Leif Simmatis,a,b Diego Guarin,c Liziane Bouvier,a,d Babak Taati,b and Yana Yunusovaa,b,d a Department of Speech-Language Pathology, Rehabilitation Sciences Institute, University of Toronto, Ontario, Canada b KITE, Toronto Rehabilitation Institute, University Health Network, Ontario, Canada c University of Florida, Gainesville d Hurvitz Brain Sciences Program, Sunnybrook Research Institute, Toronto, Ontario, Canada AR T I CLE I N F O Article History: Received June 1, 2022 Revision received September 9, 2022 Accepted January 10, 2023 Editor-in-Chief: Mili Kuruvilla-Dugdale Editor: Jun Wang https://doi.org/10.1044/2023_JSLHR-22-00321 Correspondence to Deniz Jafari: [email-redacted] . Publisher Note: This article is part of the Special Issue: Select Papers From the 2022 Conference on Motor Speech—Basic Science and Clinical Innovation. Disclosure: The authors have declared that no competing financial or nonfinancial interests existed at the time of publication. AB ST R A C T Purpose: This study sought to determine whether clinically interpretable kinematic features extracted automatically from three-dimensional (3D) videos were correlated with corresponding perceptual clinical orofacial ratings in individuals with orofacial impairments due to neurological disorders.

Method: 45 participants (19 diagnosed with motor neuron diseases [MNDs] and 26 poststroke) performed two nonspeech tasks (mouth opening and lip spreading) and one speech task (repetition of a sentence “Buy Bobby a Puppy”) while being video-recorded in a standardized lab setting. The color video recordings of participants were assessed by an expert clinician—a speech language pathologist—on the severity of three orofacial measures: symmetry, range of motion (ROM), and speed. Clinically interpretable 3D kinematic features, linked to symmetry, ROM, and speed, were automatically extracted from video recordings, using a deep facial landmark detection and tracking algorithm for each of the three tasks. Spearman correlations were used to identify features that were significantly correlated (p value < .05) with their corresponding clinical scores. Clinically significant kinematic features were then used in the subsequent multivariate regression models to predict the overall orofacial impairment severity score. Results: Several kinematic features extracted from 3D video recordings were associated with their corresponding perceptual clinical scores, indicating clinical validity of these automatically derived measures. Different patterns of significant features were observed between MND and poststroke groups; these differences were aligned with clinical expectations in both cases. Conclusions: The results show that kinematic features extracted automatically from simple clinical tasks can capture characteristics used by clinicians during assessments.

These findings support the clinical validity of video-based automatic extraction of kinematic features. Neurological disorders often affect orofacial and speech functions (Jani & Gore, 2014). For example, people poststroke (PS) or living with motor neuron diseases (MNDs) often experience orofacial motor impairments (i.e., inability to produce facial expressions or perform volitional orofacial movements), as well as dysarthria, a motor speech disorder (Flowers et al., 2013; Makkonen et al., 2016). Accurate assessment of orofacial speech and nonspeech movements becomes important for effective dis-ease detection, tracking progression, and monitoring treatment outcomes (Allison et al., 2017). Auditory-perceptual and visual assessment methods are considered gold standards for clinical differential diagnosis, assessing the severity of symptoms and functional Journal of Speech, Language, and Hearing ResearchVol. 66 3151–3165 August 2023 Copyright © 2023 American Speech-Language-Hearing Association 3151 • • •changes, as well as making treatment plans and management decisions in dysarthria in speech impairments (Bunton et al., 2007). However, these assessments tend to be subjective and heavily dependent on the clinician’s expertise. Furthermore, there are no agreed-upon standards for the detection of various disorders and monitoring of disease progression based on these methods (Allison et al., 2017; Dietsch et al., 2014; Simmons & Mayo, 1997).

Complex and expensive sensor-based techniques, such as electromagnetic articulography (EMA), have emerged as means of objective assessment, but they are not suitable for integration into current clinical workflows. Recent developments of three-dimensional (3D) video cameras and deep learning algorithms have the potential to enable accurate automatic detection and tracking of predefined facial landmarks in facial images (Bulat & Tzimiropoulos, 2017), which is the first essential step for many clinical facial analysis applications. These emerging video-based methods may offer easy-to-use and costeffective solutions to support objective clinical assessment of orofacial motor impairments. In order to be adopted into a clinical setting, novel facial tracking technologies must be evaluated within established frameworks for digital biomarker develop-ment, such as the FDA BEST (FDA-NIH Biomarker Working Group, 2016) criteria and the V3 framework (Goldsack et al., 2020). The V3 framework advocates for a three-part sequential biomarker evaluation procedure: (a) verification of sensor-level outputs, typically conducted by manufacturers, followed by (b) analytical validation of a novel method with respect to an already-validated technical gold standards, followed by (c) clinical validation to assess utility in a specific clinical population and relative to existing clinical standards/ practices. The technical verification of facial tracking technologies has been previously well established (Keselman et al., 2017; Siena et al., 2018; Zabatani et al., 2019).

The analytical validation of markerless methods has demonstrated promising results as well. Ouni and Dahmani found that facial tracking methods using two different 3D cameras, namely, Intel RealSense (Intel Corporation) and PrimeSense Carmine (PrimeSense) had good accuracy with respect to optoelectronic tracking, which functioned as a sensor-based ground truth, specifically the Vicon system (Vicon Motion Systems Ltd; Ouni & Dahmani, 2016). Bandini et al. per-formed an analogous comparison between a 3D camera (Intel RealSense) and an EMA system (NDI Wave Speech Research System), and demonstrated good accuracy of the camera compared with the Wave (Bandini, Namasivayam, & Yunusova, 2017). Harkel et al. evaluated the intra- and interrater reliability and agreement of the Intel RealSense D415 camera against a gold standard stereophotogrammetry imaging system, 3dMD, in patients with facial palsy and found that the 3D camera was a viable option to be implemented in the context of an objective clinical assessment (Harkel et al., 2020). Taken together, these previous studies have extensively demonstrated the analytical validity of 3D camera-based facial tracking methods. Beyond verification and analytical validation, the technology (both 3D camera and deep learning algorithms) must be evaluated in clinical populations in order to fulfill the third step of biomarker development.

Previous work has shown promise for detecting orofacial impairments associated with stroke (Bandini, Green, Richburg, & Yunusova, 2018), Parkinson’sd i s e a s e ( B a n d i n i et al., 2016; Bandini, Orlandi, et al., 2017), and amyotrophic lateral sclerosis (ALS; Bandini, Green, Taati, et al., 2018) relative to neurologically healthy controls, based on measures of facial kinematics (e.g., velocity, acceleration, range of motion [ROM], symmetry). For example, Bandini et al. used a small feature set capturing movement symme-try, speed, and ROM of the lips and jaw to automatically differentiate between poststroke patients and controls with 87% accuracy (Bandini, Green, Richburg, & Yunusova, 2018). In another study, a small number of 3D kinematic features of lip movement were used to discriminate patients with ALS from healthy subjects with nearly 90% accuracy (Bandini, Green, Taati, et al., 2018). Neumann et al. used a combination of kinematic features extracted from twodimensional (2D) video recordings together with acoustic features extracted from audio data to distinguish patients with ALS at symptomatic versus presymptomatic phases of bulbar disease as well as from the control participants (Neumann et al., 2021). The focus of previous studies was to investigate video-based methods’ use for classifying a group of participants into either the healthy or clinical category. The studies predicting the severity of symptoms have been limited but important in the effort to clinically validate novel assessment technologies.

One study showed that kinematics of facial expressions was able to predict the depression severity estimated via the Beck Depression Inventory– Second Edition (Pampouchidou et al., 2020). In another study, features were extracted from facial expression images of participants in order to automatically differenti-ate among five levels of pain using AI-based models evaluated its efficacy on two open-source data sets (Bargshady et al., 2020). Additionally, the Unified Parkinson’s Disease Rating Scale motor score was predicted using acoustic features extracted from 5,875 voice recordings of 42 patients with Parkinson’s disease with the final ensemble model predicting the clinical score with 99.6% accuracy (Kaur et al., 2020). These promising results highlighted the need for more research to clinically validate automated facial analysis technologies for their use as assessment tools, specifically for predicting the clinical scores associated with 3152 Journal of Speech, Language, and Hearing ResearchVol. 66 3151–3165 August 2023 severity of orofacial impairments. The ability to predict disease severity using kinematic measures extracted from videos would provide evidence of the clinical validity of the technology and support its use to enable tracking disease progression as well as evaluating the effectiveness of intervention programs (e.g., quantifying functional improvement PS).

The first objective of this study was to establish the clinical validity of a video-based system in assessing the severity of orofacial impairments by automatically extracting kinematic features from video recordings of individuals with orofacial impairments either due to stroke or MND and comparing them with respect to their corresponding clinical scores (e.g., clinical estimates of symmetry/speed/ ROM and kinematic measures of symmetry/speed/ROM). The second objective was to determine whether a combination of kinematic features was able to predict the overall severity of orofacial deficit in each patient group. We hypothesized that the selected kinematic features and corresponding perceptual clinical scores would be significantly correlated and could be used to predict the overall severity of orofacial impairment. Method Participants Data from 45 patient participants were included in this study: 19 individuals who were diagnosed with MND (including ALS = 16; primary lateral sclerosis = 1; Kennedy’s disease = 2) and 26 individuals who experienced a stroke (PS). We combined all MND patients into the same group as the manifestation of the disease in the orofacial mechanism was expected to be similar (e.g., resulting primarily in kinematic changes due to muscle weakness; Clark et al., 2022). Table 1 presents the demographic and clinical representation of the samples. All participants were cognitively unimpaired (Montreal Cognitive Assessment score ≥ 26; Nasreddine et al., 2005) and passed a hearing screening at 25 dB for both ears at 500, 1000, 2000, and 4000 Hz.

The MND group was also evaluated via ALS Functional Rating Scale–Revised (Cedarbaum et al., 1999). The study was approved by the research ethics boards at the Sunnybrook Research Institute and University Health Network: Toronto Rehabilitation Institute, Toronto, Canada. All participants provided informed consent according to the requirements of the Declaration of Helsinki. Table 1. Clinical Assessment A highly experienced speech-language pathologist (SLP) specializing in the assessment of adults with neuro-logical diseases (> 10 years of clinical experience) watched the available video recordings and rated them based on clinical measures of ROM, speed, symmetry, variability, and fatigue of facial movements. The rater only knew the diagnosis of the participants prior to performing the assessment and otherwise was blind to all other clinical information (e.g., scores on other clinical assessments, site of onset, disease duration). Ratings were based on a 5point Likert scale with 1 indicating normal function and 5 indicating severe dysfunction.

ROM, speed, and symmetry scores were used as separate clinical outcomes; they were selected as they constitute salient neuromuscular abnormalities in motor speech disorders (Duffy, 2019). The total score—a sum of all five clinical ratings—was calculated and used to represent the overall orofacial deficit severity, with score of 5 indicating normal function and score of 25 indicating the most severe dysfunction (see Bandini et al., 2021). To determine the reliability of the clinician scores, the original examinator (intrarater reliability) and another trained examinator (a certified SLP; interrater reliability) rescored videos from 15% of the sample (seven participants chosen randomly; 21 recordings). To assess intrarater reliability, intraclass correlation coefficient (ICC) estimates and their 95% confidence intervals (CIs) were calculated using Statistical Package for the Social Sciences (SPSS) Version 23 (SPSS Inc.) based on a single-rating, absolute-agreement, two-way mixed-effects model (McGraw & Wong, 1996). To assess interrater agreement, ICC esti-mates and their 95% CI were calculated based on a singlerating, consistency-agreement, two-way random-effects model (McGraw & Wong, 1996). A percentage of clinical agreement (percentage of scores within 1-point difference) was also calculated to mitigate the effects of skewed distributions or low variability of the scores in ICC values, if Deniz et al.: Clinical Validation of Orofacial Assessment Tool 3153 • • •necessary. The intrarater agreement was strong (ICC = .966, CI [0.908, 0.987], p < .001; Koo & Li, 2016).

The interrater agreement was also excellent (ICC = .996, CI [0.990, 0.998], p < .001; Koo & Li, 2016). All scores fell within 1-point difference for both intrarater and interrater reliability. Data Collection Procedure Video recordings were obtained while participants were seated in front of an Intel RealSense depth camera (SR300 or D400) at approximately 30–60 cm distance away from the face at the eye level height. The recordings were performed in a quiet room with a single source uniform light illuminating the participants’ face. Each video recording was composed of a pair of color (RGB) and depth videos. Both streams were recorded simultaneously at approximately 50 frames per second at VGA resolution of 640 × 480 pixels. Participants were asked to perform a set of standard speech and nonspeech tasks commonly used by clinicians during orofacial and motor speech examinations to assess the severity of orofacial symptoms and dysarthria. The subjects were asked to avoid head and body movements and look directly into the camera during recordings.

The following set of tasks were chosen for analysis as they captured the orofacial movements of mouth and jaw in both horizontal and vertical directions and were affected in both clinical populations in our previous studies (Bandini, Green, Richburg, & Yunusova, 2018; Bandini, Green, Taati, et al., 2018; Bandini, Green, Wang, et al., 2018): • 10 repetitions of the sentence “Buy Bobby a Puppy” at a comfortable speaking rate and loudness (BBP); • five repetitions of maximum jaw opening and closing (OPEN); and • five repetitions of pretending to smile with tight lips (SPREAD). Furthermore, we recorded 10 s of participants resting, while they maintained a neutral facial expression (REST) with their teeth together but not clenched. This was done for distance normalization purposes. A total of 175 video recordings were included in the final analysis: 74 from MND and 101 from PS. A total of five recordings were either missing or excluded. Reasons for exclusion of a video recording for a task were participant not performing the task (two cases—in MND group, BBP task); video recording issues, for example, corruption of either the color and/or depth stream data of video recordings, or dropping of video frames (two cases—in PS group, BBP task); and failed accurate facial landmark tracking due to poor lighting condition (one case—in PS group, BBP task). Each of the remaining videos, excluding the data for the REST task, were manually segmented into individual repetitions by a trained research assistant (i.e., five segments for the nonspeech tasks and 10 seg-ments for the speech task).

Kinematic Data Analysis Preprocessing A fine-tuned version of the face alignment network (FAN; Bulat & Tzimiropoulos, 2017) was used to extract 68 facial landmarks from color videos in 2D pixel values and track them throughout the duration of the videos (see Figure 1). The 3D positions of landmarks were then calculated by aligning and sampling the depth images at the same pixel coordinates as the landmark points identified on the color images. The 3D real world coordinates of the landmarks (in meters) were calculated using the intrinsic camera parameters together with aligning the depth and color streams, thus, obtaining 3D coordinates for each landmark, with X representing the lateral axis, Y representing the vertical axis, and Z representing the frontal axis. To address this issue, the model was previously fine-tuned using 4,340 images extracted from video recordings of individuals from clinical populations as well as healthy older adults, some of which were of a subset of the individuals in this study, performing orofacial tasks (Guarin, Bandini, et al., 2020). The normalized root mean square error of the fine-tuned model was reported to be 4.7 ± 0.7%, 6.2 ± 1.7%, and 5.7 ± 1.1% for healthy control, PS group, and ALS group, respectively (Guarin, Bandini, et al., 2020). The fine-tuned FAN model has been previously used to suc-cessfully detect oromotor impairments associated with various neurological disorders (Bandini et al., 2021; Guarin, Yunusova, et al., 2020).

To check the model’s accuracy, a video file was generated of participants performing eac h task with the location of 2D landmarks superimposed on their faces. Each video was viewed to ensure adequate detection and tracking of facial landmarks of interest. Out of 68 facial landmarks, 10 landmarks from lips and jaw were considered in this study. They included forehead (FH), left medial canthus of eye (LE), right medial canthus of eye (RE), upper vermillion lip border at mouth center (UL), lower vermillion lip border (LL) at mouth center, left oral commissure (LC), right oral commissure (RC), center jawline, left jawline (LJ), and right jawline (RJ). Figure 1 visualizes the 68 facial landmarks and the 10 landmarks chosen for the study to examine movements of the lips and jaw. To remove tracking noise and jitter, the trajectories of the landmarks of the interests were lowpass filtered with an eight-pole Butterworth filter with cutoff frequency of 12 Hz (Bandini et al., 2021; Bandini, Green, et al., 2017). Kinematic Features Similar to previous studies, facial landmarks were used to derive clinically interpretable and relevant kinematic features (Bandini, Green, Taati, et al., 2018; Guarin, Bandini, et al., 2020). These features are summarized in Table 2. Features were selected to correspond to clinical judgments of ROM, speed, and symmetry by the SLP. A total of 20 kinematic features were linked to ROM, 24 to speed, and nine to symmetry. All the features have been used in previous studies (Bandini, Green, Wang, et al., 2018; Bandini, Orlandi, et al., 2017).

ROM Features The 3D Euclidean distances between a set of facial landmarks were calculated to establish lip opening, UL – LL; lip spread, RC – LC; movement of LL with respect to the FH reference point; movement of RC with respect to RE; and movement of LC with respect to LE. In addition, the total movement of the mouth was modeled by a sum of the areas made by two triangles defined by lip landmarks (RC, UL, LL) and (LC, UL, LL). Lip spread as well as other RC/LC measures were established only for SPREAD, where horizontal movements were predomi-nant. Since the jaw rarely moves during SPREAD, none of the jaw-based features were considered for this task. All the calculated distance features were normalized with respect to the corresponding values calculated in the REST condition. After this normalization, ROM was determined as the maximum value of each of these distances/areas, as well as the cumulative sum path length. This was done for each repetition. Speed Features The first derivative of the above path distances with respect to time were calculated to represent the velocity of the lips and jaw movements. The maximum, minimum, and average values were considered. Symmetry Features The symmetry of movement was characterized by (a) comparing the motion of the left and right corners of the mouth with respect to the reference points LE and RE and (b) comparing the areas on the right and left triangles defined by (RC, UL, LL) and (LC, UL, LL), respectively.

The absolute differences, ratios, and concordance correlation coefficient (CCC; Lin et al., 2002; as a measure of agreement of two signals) between the computed values of the distances and mouth areas on the right and left sides were calculated. Smaller CCC values corresponded to asymmetry during facial movements. Similarly, the larger deviation of right/left ratios away from 1 indicated more asymmetrical facial movements. Statistical Analyses The features described above were calculated for individual repetitions of all tasks. Prior to further analyses, outliers were removed from the data using the interquartile range (IQR) method (Dovoedo & Chakraborti, 2015). In this method, the IQR of the repetitions were calculated, then multiplied by 2.5, and added/subtracted from the third/first quartiles. The values per repetition of each task that fell outside this range were considered outliers and were eliminated from the set. In total, 4.7% of the repetitions were discarded as outliers (n = 51). The arithmetic mean of kinematic feature values were calculated across the remaining repetitions per subject, per task. All subsequent analyses were conducted separately for each task (OPEN, SPREAD, and BBP) and group (MND vs. PS). The Spearman correlation coefficients were calculated between each kinematic feature and their corresponding Deniz et al.: Clinical Validation of Orofacial Assessment Tool 3155 • • •perceptual clinical scores.

The Spearman rho (ρ)i su s e d with ordinal data and measures the degree of monotonic relationship between two variables; here, in accordance with accepted guidelines, values between 0 to ±0.20 were considered as negligible, ±0.21 to ±0.40 as weak, ±0.41 to ±0.60 as moderate, ±0.61 to ±0.80 as strong, and ±0.81 to ±1.00 as very strong (Prion & Haerling, 2014). The significance level for each correlation was set to .05 to reflect that these correlat ions were primarily used as a screening method for subsequent regression analyses. Table 2. Kinematic features with statistically significant correlations were included in the subsequent multivariable linear regression models with the total orofacial clinical score as the outcome. Prior to the analysis, continuous covariates were standardized to provide consistency across regression coefficients (Oka, 2021). The variance inflation factor (VIF) with threshold of greater than 10 was used to assess the collinearity between covariates (Marcoulides & Raykov, 2019).

If the VIF for the variables were higher than the set threshold, the features with highest VIF were eliminated iteratively until the remaining variables all had VIF values less than 10. The normality of residuals was tested by plotting the Q-Q norm of the residuals. The linearity of fitted regression models was also evaluated by plotting the residual values against the kinematic features data. The adjusted R 2 , adjusted for the number of predictors, was reported for the final fitted regression models. 3156 Journal of Speech, Language, and Hearing ResearchVol. 66 3151–3165 August 2023 Results Clinical Scores Figure 2 shows the distribution of the severity of the clinical scores for each orofacial task and participant group. Notably, the variation of the clinical scores was task dependent; a larger spread of all scores was noted for the SPREAD task in both groups and to some extent in BBP, particularly for the MND group. Correlation Analyses Between Clinical Scores and Kinematic Measures Tables 3, 4, and 5 present the Spearman correlation coefficients between the extracted 3D orofacial kinematic features and their corresponding perceptual scores of ROM, speed, and symmetry, respectively, for each task (OPEN, SPREAD, and BBP) separately. A set of features in each clinical group showed moderate to good correlation with the perceptual clinical scores given by the expert clinician.

In the OPEN task, kinematic features of ROM and speed (seven features in total) showed significant correla-tions with the corresponding clinical scores only in the MND group, while none of the features were significant for the PS group. A total of 16 kinematic measures (eight for MND and eight for PS) showed significant correlations with clinical scores with the majority of measures in the speed category. For the BBP task, only one ROM feature had a statistically significant correlation with the clinical measure of ROM in the MND group; at the same time 15 kinematic features, mostly in the ROM and symmetry category, were statistically significant in the PS group. The statistically significant features within each task and clinical subgroup were representative of clinical characteristics anticipated by the group. Figure 2. In the PS case, a single kinematic feature (vSpread_Max) was eliminated for SPREAD, and seven features were eliminated for BBP (Open_Max, LL_Max, LL_Path, RJ_Max, RJ_Path, LJ_Path, and vLL_Max).

Table 6 presents the regression coefficients that predicted the total clinical scores based on kinematic features for the MND group. For OPEN, kinematic features predicted the overall clinical score with adjusted R2 value of .50 (p < .01), and five out of six kinematic predictors were statistically significant. For SPREAD, the adjusted R2 was .35 (p < .01); for BBP, it was 0.11 (p ~ .09). No individual features were significant for the SPREAD or BBP models. Table 7 presents regression results for the PS group. For SPREAD, the adjusted R2 value was .57 (p < .01) and significant predictors included: vSpread_Min, vRC_Min, and RCLC_CCC. For BBP, the adjusted R2 value was .54 (p < .01), and significant predictors were tA_Max and vO_Max. A regression model was not built for the OPEN task as none of the features had statistically significant correlations with their individual corresponding clinical scores. Discussion In this exploratory study, we investigated clinical validity of a 3D camera and vision-based facial tracking algorithm as an objective assessment tool for patients with neurological disorders—MND and PS. A relatively small, clinically interpretable lip and jaw kinematic feature set was delineated from the outset; the features were chosen to conceptually represent the measures used by clinicians to assess orofacial deficits during the oromotor examina-tions of nonspeech and speech tasks. The study revealed that subsets of kinematic features were significantly correlated with their corresponding clinical scores for each clinical group.

The sets of statistically significant features that were able to explain a significant portion of data variability in the overall severity scores in most models were unique for each of the clinical subgroups. Overall, the findings demonstrated the potential of automated videobased systems for extraction of kinematic features in the Deniz et al.: Clinical Validation of Orofacial Assessment Tool 3157 • • •context of orofacial neurological assessment and established their initial validity as clinical tools. However, the findings of this study aligned strongly with those of previous work with regards to facial kinematics and oromotor phenomena in both MND and PS groups. In the MND group, our findings were consistent with previous literature describing abnormal jaw and lip movements (Bandini, Green, et al., 2017; Bandini, Green, Taati, et al., 2018; Yunusova et al., 2008, 2010).

MND has been characterized by weakness in the orofacial musculature (Clark et al., 2022) and, expectedly, ROM and speed measures were noted to be substantially more affected by MND than symmetry (see Shellikeri et al., 2016). As a result, we observed significant correlations between the clinical scores indicating the impairment severity and kinematic features primarily related to ROM and speed. In the PS group, facial paresis, resulting in facial asymmetry with a unilateral muscle impairment of the lower half of the face, causing asymmetric smile and other facial gestures, is one of the most common impairments following a stroke (Bandini, Green, Richburg, & Yunusova, 2018; Konecny et al., 2011). As anticipated, the symmetry features played a prominent role in the PS group, and our finding showed significant correlations between kinematically assessed symmetry and the corresponding symmetry clinical scores. In both clinical groups, the agreement between clinical characteristics sought by experts to describe the unique clinical representation of orofacial deficits and corresponding kinematic features supported the overall impression of clinical validity of the video-based movement assessment. Task Differences Differences also emerged in this study between tasks. In the MND group, kinematic measures of ROM and speed were linked to the corresponding clinical scores in nonspeech OPEN and SPREAD tasks, but not in the BBP speech task. The findings for nonspeech tasks were anticipated based on past research (Bandini, Green, Taati, et al., 2018).

Both tasks are classified as maximum perfor-mance tasks, yet clinically they serve different purposes. While OPEN primarily is used to judge the integrity of jaw depressor/elevator muscles, SPREAD is used to judge Deniz et al.: Clinical Validation of Orofacial Assessment Tool 3159 • • •the integrity of intrinsic facial muscles. Previously, kinematic features extracted from the lower lip and jaw in SPREAD and OPEN were used to differentiate patients with ALS from healthy control participants with 83% and 72% accuracy, respectively (Bandini, Green, Taati, et al., 2018). The findings regarding the speech task were somewhat unexpected, as previous studies had shown BBP to be highly sensitive to the presence of the MND (Bandini, Green, et al., 2017; Ban dini, Green, Wang, et al., 2018). Current results for BBP may be due to the challenges associated with the clinical (visuo-perceptual) assessment of speech versus nonspeech movements by SLPs. BBP is a relatively fast task and as such it might be more challeng-ing for clinicians to rank its clinical characteristics (ROM, speed, and symmetry) on a 5-point scale. In the PS group, results for the vertical opening (OPEN) contrasted with the horizontal opening (SPREAD) and speech (BBP) task. None of the kinematic features were correlated with clinical scores in OPEN, while sets of features were correlated with the clinical scores in SPREAD and BBP. The lack of correlation among fea-tures and the clinical scores in OPEN may be due to the task being mainly used to assess the integrity of the jaw (i.e., cranial nerve V) and not facial muscles, which might be differentially affected PS (Bandini, Green, Richburg, & Yunusova, 2018). The SPREAD task showed more notable effect as it intended to reveal the integrity of facial muscles (i.e., cranial nerve VII). Although relatively mild, this type of impairment predominated in our PS sample. Facial features during BBP also revealed a link with the clinical symmetry score in these patients. Similar results for SPREAD and BBP with respect to their sensitivity to orofacial impairment PS were reported in previous work (Bandini, Green, Richburg, & Yunu-sova, 2018).

Overall, with the exception of BBP task in the MND group, task effects observed clinically and kinematically in this study demonstrated results consistent with previous research and, by extension, supported the potential for clinical validity of the tested assessment method. Modeling Overall Orofacial Impairment Severity A combination of kinematic features was able to predict the overall orofacial impairment severity score in each clinical group and some tasks. This was important to examine as the overall severity score combined clinical impressions across different specific observations that might be difficult to rank separately and individually. In the MND group, significant models were noted for OPEN and SPREAD. All but one individual variable (LL_Max) contributed to the overall model in OPEN explaining a substantial degree of variability of the total clinical score. The value of the coefficients generally suggested that as the severity increased, the values of the ROM and speed of the lips and jaw were reduced. The SPREAD model was significant, but Deniz et al.: Clinical Validation of Orofacial Assessment Tool 3161 • • •none of the kinematic variables significantly contributed to explaining the model’s overall variance. This may suggest that the SPREAD was not an ideal task for feature extraction in this group, as previous research suggested that the greatest impairment in MND affects the vertical movement plane of the jaw and lips (Yunusova et al., 2010). In the PS group, in contrast with MND, most of the features were significant in the regression model for SPREAD, with kinematic features explaining over half of the variance in the data. The results of this study are promising as the variability of the kinematic measures within and in between subjects in the study were consider-able. The value of the coefficients revealed that as the severity increased, the lip movement became more reduced and asymmetrical. In the BBP task, total mouth are a and the maximum mouth opening velocity were the two statistically significant features with the model being able to explain substantial amount of the variance in the data. These features align with clinicians’ judgment as they assess the overall mouth movement and speed of movement during a fast task such as BBP. Overall, the features related to symmetrical movement of the mouth demonstrated to be of the most importance aligned with the clinical perceptual assessment of this clinical group, further supporting the potential use of this method for future automatic objective assessment. Limitations and Future Directions The present work had limitations to acknowledge and to address in future studies. The primary limitation was a relatively small sample size and differences in sample sizes of the two clinical groups, limiting our ability to fully compare group results.

Small sample sizes remain an issue for many clinicals studies pre and during the pandemic, which expedited the transition of this type of work into the domain of remote data collections/assessment, for which this study results are of a particular importance. Furthermore, the intra- and interrater reliability was performed on a small subsample (15% of the sample). Although it is relatively standard to evaluate approximately that amount of data to establish reliability, the sample there may have been small, introducing the possibility of perceptual bias. Nevertheless, we observed small CIs in the reliability analyses, which supported the adequacy of this method for its present purposes (i.e., evaluating the performance of raters at a sample level, rather than at a participant level). 3162 Journal of Speech, Language, and Hearing ResearchVol. 66 3151–3165 August 2023 Another limiting factor was a relatively small severity range in both clinical samples. We capitalized on the greater variability available in our samples based on specific tasks instead. In addition, the kinematic features extracted were averaged across the repetitions for each subject per task and thus variability of the facial movements during each repetition of the task was not considered. This work performed analysis on 3D video data (color and depth) alone. Recent studies have shown that multimodal data (both video and audio) can further improve the predictive power of such algorithms (Chmielińska et al., 2020; Neumann et al., 2021; Palliya Guruge et al., 2021; Park et al., 2020).

Similarly, future work, particularly in analyzing speech tasks, could benefit from extracting clinically valid speech features from audio recordings of the speech tasks to potentially increase the predictive power of the regression models. Future work will extend the results obtained in this study, while focusing on gathering data from a larger/ balanced group of participants with more severe orofacial impairments as well as developing predictive models using time series of facial landmarks instead of averaging kinematic feature values across repetitions. This work will be essential to develop and integrate modern digital technologies into clinical care as well as outcome measures for clinical trials (Goldsack et al., 2021). This study laid the foundation for larger scale studies with larger sample size across different cohorts to ensure clinical validity of the proposed technology as well as generalizability of the results. Conclusions The result of this study showed that the automatic extraction of facial kinematic features from facial video recordings of patients performing standard orofacial assessments is a viable solution for an objective and continuous assessment of facial function. Here, we demonstrated encouraging support for the validity of our automated assessment method for detecting the severity of kinematic facial impairments.

Development of such sys-tems and their validation with larger data sets holds great promise for the development of clinical tools that can help clinicians objectively assess oromotor and speech disorder and track progression of neurological diseases. 3D VIDEO TRACKING TECHNOLOGY IN THE ASSESSMENT OF OROFACIAL IMPAIRMENTS IN NEUROLOGICAL DISORDERS By Deniz Jafari A thesis submitted in conformity with the requirements for the degree of Master of Science Rehabilitation Sciences Institute University of Toronto © Copyright 2022 by Deniz Jafari ii 3D video tracking technology in the assessment of orofacial impairments in neurological Disorders Deniz Jafari Master of Science Rehabilitation Sciences Institute University of Toronto 2022 Abstract & Keywords Changes to the orofacial muscles’ movement and speech are often among the earliest signs perceived in Neurological disorders . Detection of subtle changes in speech and facial movements can help with the diagnosis and prognosis of neurological disorders . Deep artificial intelligent video -based facial analysis models have the potential to be used as objective and non-invasive clinical tools . This thesis used the V3 framework for evaluation of digital biomarkers and their adoption into clinical settings to evaluat e an automatic video -based facial analysis system as an objective assessment tool for accessing orofacial movements. The proposed system consists of a 3D camera and Artificial Intelligent -based algorithms that automatically extract objective clinically interpretable kinematic features from video recordings of individuals performing standard orofacial tasks. This work investigates the analytical and clinical validation of the proposed system to assess the severity of oro facial impairment in clinical groups .

In fact, it is estimated that the older adults aged 60 and over will account for an estimated 22% of global population by year 2050 [2]. Despite the above -mentioned improvements in the life expectancy, neurological conditions have arguably become the most dreaded condition of older adults, with age bei ng the main risk factor for such disorders, including Alzheimer disease (AD), Parkinson disease (PD), motor neuron disease (MND), and stroke [3]. Treating and caring for individuals with such conditions are costly and expected to multiply in the years to come. It is estimated that caring for older adults with neurological conditions already consumed over $600 billion worldwide [4].

As the global population of older adults is on the rise, so is the prevalence of age -related neurological conditions and th e need for development of cheap, efficient, scalable tools that are sensitive to the early signs and subtle changes associated with the onset of neurological disorders. Neurological disorders are brain, cognitive, or behavioural disorders that may affect various human functions such as speaking, limb movements and cognitive/ l earning capacity. There are over six hundred known neurological impairments with the global prevalence of these diseases found to be at 10.2% [5]. The most common observed impairme nts in neurological conditions are impaired motor, sensory, visual, balance, cognition, swallowing function and communication. Motor impairment s include weakness o r complete paralysis across various muscle groups e.g., in the muscles of upper or lower limb s, trunk muscles as well as muscles of the head and neck , including orofacial muscles . Sensory impairment includes numbness, loss of sensation of touch, pressure, or pain the affected body structures . Cognitive impairment includes , as example, deficits in attention, memory, or visuo -spatial orientation. A common sign of neurological disorders is the loss or altered speech and inability to eat / swallow normally . Communication impairments may be due to aphasia, 2 dysarthria, or apraxia of speech [6]. Dysarthria and apraxia of speech represent the defects to the motor aspects of speech production [6], [7] .

Clinical assessment of dysarthria Changes in one’s speech might be among one of the early signs of a neurological disorder [8]. Dysarthria - a motor speech disorder – is due to weakness, slowness, or incoordination of the musculature used to produce speech [6], [9] . Dysarthria is the most common occurring speech disorder in neurological conditions, with observed occurrence of 51% in patients with multiple sclerosis, 20 -30% in individuals post-stroke, and 50 -89% of PD patients [6]. Speech difficulties are observed at disease onset in approximately 30% of patients with Amyotrophic Lateral Sclerosis (ALS - aka bulbar onset ALS); while in 80 -95% of ALS patients, dysarthria is developed as the disease progresses [10]. Auditory -perceptual assessment methods are considered gold standards for clinical differential diagnosis, assessing severity of symptoms and functional changes as well as making treatment plans and management decisions in dysarthria [11]. For example, a system developed by Darley, Aronson, and Brown established 38 perceptual dimensions defining auditory perceptual changes in respiration, voice, articulation, prosody, and other aspects of speech to characterize the dysarthria type and localize the lesion s ite [11]. Auditory -perceptual methods have great advantage such as convenience, economy, and robustness , however, they have been also criticized for being susceptible to various sources of error and biases [12].

This method is heavily subjective and fac tors such as listener’s shifting internal standards, past experiences and area of expertise, and type of rating scale used may become confounding factors [13]. Assessment of orofacial impairments Orofacial muscles are often impaired in neurological diseases. For example, patients with PD can suffer from hypomimia, also known as masked face, in which they have reduced range and speed of facial muscle movements. In order to diagnose this condition, n eurologists use the Movement Disorder Society – Unified Parkinson’s Disease Rating Scale (MDS -UPDRS) to evaluate the presence and the severity of impairments. Section III of the assessment includes items that specifically evaluate the patients’ facial expr essions [14]. ALS patients have various orofacial 3 and speech difficulties that are observed in 80 -95% of patients as the disease progresses with reduced speaking rate and speech intelligibility being identified as the worst aspects of the disease [10]. The ALS Functional Rating Scale – Revised (ALSFRS -R) is a 12 -item questionnaire that allows patients to rate their current motor functions including speech, swallowing, and saliva management. It is a highly reliable and easily administrable system that ha ve been used by clinicians to keep track of disease progression [15]. In stroke patients, the orofacial impairments depend on lesion’s size and affected cerebral structure s. Orofacial impairments may occur due to impairments of the cranial nerves, centra l nervous system pathways, or motoneuron pool.

Stroke patients have demonstrated reduced tongue and lip forces. Facial asymmetry is also common in this clinical group [16]. The cranial nerve examination aims at providing information regarding the focal point of the pathology in the brain or brainstem [17]. It includes examining the functionality of the nerves that innervate muscles of the head and neck. The cranial nerves can be separated into four major groups: the sensory nerves, nerves responsible o f eye movements, nerves of oral cavity and superior pharynx, and those responsible of neck movements . The neurological examination of these nerves includes palpation, check for symmetry and wasting, and testing for weaknesses or paralysis of muscles. The e xamination includes assessment of structures at rest, during nonspeech activities for example during sustained postures and movement s as well as during speech tasks, and in response to reflex testing [18]. However, studies have shown that the assessments performed by the experts are subjective and heavily dependent on the clinician’s expertise . Further, there are no agreed upon standards for detection of various disorders and monitoring of disease progression among experts in the field [15], [1 9], [20] . Using technology to support clinical assessment With the advent of technology and increased availability of sensor -based solution to the consumer marker together with an attempt to overcome the limitations of clinical assessments, instrumentat ion-based protocols have been explored for the assessment of speech and orofacial impairments [21]–[23].

Some of these instrumented systems include those based on acoustic analyses (e.g., Phonatory aerodynamic system (PAS), nasometer ) or kinematic/ movement -based methods (e.g., 3D optical motion capture system, and 3D electromagnetic articulography (EMA)) . 4 Kinematic methods are of particular interest because they allow precise measurements of the movement associated with speech and orofacial functions. The 3D optical motion capture system uses infrared light to track radio reflective markers that are placed on specific facial landmarks to track the facial movement during speech and non speech tasks. The facial kinematics data such as range of motion and speed of each articulator provides valuable insight on the performance of the lips, jaw and other facial muscles [22]. This technology however requires a direct line -of-sight between the ma rker and the camera and cannot be used for the assessment of tongue. As a result, electromagnetic devices , technology based on electromagnetic field that does not require direct line -of-sight , have been used to track and assess the movement of the tongue as well as the lips, jaw and face during speech and orofacial assessments [24]. Studies using EMA have shown promising result s and higher sensitivity to the early subtle changes/symptoms in patients relative to healthy adults compared to both clinicians and patients’ self -reports [21], [25] –[28].

However, such systems have their own challenges that hinder their adoption into cl inical settings including: their complexity and need of further specialized training for clinicians to be able to analyze and understand the data, the high cost associated with the instrumentation, and the long setup time of the sensor -based systems that r esult in patient fatigue and prolonged assessment time [15]. Solution: 3D cameras The rapid development of 3D sensing technology in the past decade has resulted in many consumer applications. The Microsoft KinectTM was among the first 3D sensing consume r products released that enabled new gaming experience. This product used a depth sensor developed by Primesense that was based on structured light technology. Many other 3D sensing devices shortly followed, some examples were Intel RealSense 3D camera range, Intel® Euclid™ (Intel Corp., Santa Clara, CA, USA), Creative Senz3D (Creative Technology Ltd., Singapore), Asus Xtion Pro Live (AsusTek Computer Inc., Beitou District, Taipei, Taiwan), OptiTrack MoCap (NaturalPoint, Inc., USA), Vicon Infineon 3D Image Sensor (Vicon Motion Systems Ltd., Oxford, UK), and Kinect V2 (Microsoft, Redmond, WA, USA ) [29]. As a result, various applications started integrating these technologies , such as gesture and action recognition [30], [31] , facial expression analysis [32], and marker -less motion capture [33] just to name a few. 5 Intel RealSense 3D camera w as one of the first low -cost, compact with high image quality that was widely adopted in the research community.

Intel RealSense 3D camera consists of a depth camera (a projector and a sensor for triangulation) and an RGB camera that is full HD with 1920×1080 pixels resolution. The projector emits 860 nm near -infrared range light, and the IR sensor has a 640×480 pixels resolution with f rame rate of 600 fps (1200 fps in half resolution). It is capable of providing a VGA -size depth map at 60 frames per second with 0.125 mm depth resolution, i.e., submillimeter accuracy [34]. There are numerous studies that have explored the use of such 3D cameras for clinical applications. In rehabilitation, such technologies have been considered for enabling patient feedback and correction while performing exercise regimens. For example, Chhor et al. [35] developed a computer game that used Intel RealSense 3D c amera to aid upper body rehabilitation by tracking hand gestures. Other examples of applications using Intel RealSense 3D camera by tracking facial expression, movement of joints and skeletal tracking [29], [36], [37] . The depth sensing technology has al so been used in facial analysis for tasks such as emotion recognition [38] and providing outcome measures for the assess ment of neurological disorders in various clinical populations [39]–[43]. It is the latter application that has a great potential fo r integration into everyday clinical practice – for in clinic and remote assessments – as well as into clinical trials of pharmaceutical and behavioural interventions.

Facial Landmark Detection The development of modern artificial intelligence (AI) and de ep learning techniques – specifically, automated video analysis algorithms – afford substantial advances using video cameras for clinical assessment and monitoring. Facial landmark detection and tracking algorithms attempt to automatically detect the locat ion of key facial landmarks on facial images or videos and are the building block of many applications including facial recognition, animation, tracking, expression analysis, and 3D facial modeling. Facial landmark detection is a challenging task for reaso ns such as diverse facial appearances across subjects, expression and pose changes of a given subject, partial occlusion and environmental factors ( e.g., illumination). Therefore, earlier algorithms aimed at detecting landmarks in controlled conditions, fo r example under a fixed laboratory setting , with fixed head pose and minimal or highly specific facial expression s. In recent 6 years, more research has been focused on in -the-wild conditions, where algorithms aim at having a robust performance for arbitrary facial expression s, head pose s, environmental conditions, etc. [44], [45] . Facial landmark detection algorithm s are generally supervised ; they use a large set of manually labelled training facial images , from which the AI model learns to locate the same landmarks on future test face images. These models first use a face detector to obtain a rectangular bounding bo x of the face within a given image.

Depending on the model , different number of facial landmarks are outputted ( e.g., a 17-point model, 29 -point model, or 68 -point model) marking the locations of the eyes, nose, mouth etc. on the face. The output is in a f orm of vector x = (x 1, . . . , xN , y1 , . . . , y N)T where (x i , yi), which denotes the pixel values of the ith landmark in the image , refer to Figure 1-1. Figure 1-1 Output of facial landmark detection algorithm , the pixel values of x and y coordinates are superimposed on the input face image Facial landmark detection models prior to deep learning are categorized into two primary categories , based on either parametric or nonparametric methods. The parametric meth ods are built based on data belonging to a particular distribution such as a Gaussian mixture model, while

primary categories , based on either parametric or nonparametric methods. The parametric meth ods are built based on data belonging to a particular distribution such as a Gaussian mixture model, while

7 nonparametric models do not assume a distribution. The parametric models have a fixed set of parameters that are tuned based on the input data . Gauss ian mixture models and the Active Appearance Models are the most commo n methods used in this category [46]–[50]. In contrast, t he number of parameters in the nonparametric models may vary based on the number and complexity of the training data ; these models may still have implicit assumptions or shape constraints built in because of the specifics of the training data. Graphical models such as t ree structures and Markov random field methods , in whic h the location of each facial landmark is optimally solved using dynamic programming , are examples of nonparametric methods [51]. In recent years, deep networks, e.g., convolutional neural network (CNN), have shown superior performance for facial landma rk detection. These methods learn the facial shape and appearance variation s and the nonlinear mapping from facial images to landmark coordinates. Examples include deep hierarchical face parsing [52] and coarse -to-fine autoencoder networks [53]. In general , deep neural net-based algorithms are divided into two main categories of direct regression methods that directly predict the coordinates of each landmark (e.g.

[54]–[58]) and heatmap regression -based methods , where a 2D heatmap is generated for location of each landmark (e.g. [59], [60] ) [61]. Some of the common architectures used as building block s for either of the above -mentioned approaches are ResNet [58], MobileNet [57], ShuffleNet [56], Hourglass [59], HRNet [62], and CU-Net [60]. The most commonly used architecture is hourglass architecture, initially introduced for pose estimation [63], which is an encoder -decoder network block that can be sequentially stacked [59]. In facial video analysis, recurrent neural networks (RNN) [64] and long short -term memory (LSTM) [65] architectures have been used to minimize frame -to-frame landmark jitter. Based on a review conducted on the state -of-the-art facial landmark detection algorithms developed in recent years [61], some of the best performing models on various datasets include SubpixelHeatmap [66], ADNet [67], and PropagationNet [68]. In SubpixelHeatmap, the authors use local soft-argmax computation to reduce the quantization error of the generated heatmap [66]. The authors for ADNet use the fact that the error in the tangent direction (relative to face boundary) in general ha s higher value than the error in the normal direction , thus, design the architecture such that it assign s higher weights to normal error [67]. In PropagationNet, the authors note the importance of facial boundary for accurate detection of the facial landmarks [68].

In this approach , convolutional 8 blocks transform landmark heatmaps into boundary heatmaps after each Hourglass module and use it as an intermediate attention mask to improve final accuracy predictions [68]. There are several publicly available datasets for training facial landmark detection models [69]–[72]. These dataset s can be categorized into those captured in controlled conditions ( e.g., in a lab setting with fixed head pose and facial expression ) [71], [73] , or those captured in uncontrolled envir onment s, i.e., in-the-wild [70]. The latter are often collection of images from websites such as Facebook or Google images. The ground truth facial landmarks are obtained manually by employing workers or crowdsourcing through services such as the Amazon Mechanical Turk. Each image is generally labelled by several workers and the averaged labelled values are taken as final ground truth of landmark positions. The accuracy of the models is measured by computing t he distance between the landmark positions predicted by a landmark detection model and the ground truth labels . The computed distance is commonly normalized by the interocular distance and is referred to as the normalized mean error (NME) [61]. Another metric used to report model performance is the failure rate (FR) , which denotes the number of images with NME above a given (e.g. 10%) threshold [61].

Facial landmark detection and tracking is an important first step ( i.e., a preprocessing step) for many application areas such as face recognition, expression and faci al movement analysis, as well as pain detection [74]–[78]. Exploring various algorithms and deep learning architectures and methodologies to improve the tracking accuracy remains an active area of research. Currently, the focus o f the field is to balance accuracy and complexity of the models being developed as well as leveraging semi -supervised and unsupervised learning methods in order to take advantage of the abundant unlabeled content online to more effectively train the models [79]. The Face Alignm ent Network The Face Alignment Network (FAN) [80] is a deep neural network with residual blocks trained on a large pool of real and synthetically expanded images with ground truth facial landmark locations. The FAN architecture is composed of four sequential components , each with an hourglass (HG) architecture [63], in which the bottleneck blocks were replaced with hierarchical, parallel , and multiscale blocks [81]. The outputs are 68 heatmaps of 64×64 dimensions , each of 9 which estimates the position of a facial landmark. The 2D -FAN was trained using 230,000 images in the training set and was evaluated on 7,200 images in the test set [80]. In previous orofacial analysis studies , FAN was used to extract facial landmarks in clinical populations [82]–[85]. Aligned with findings from Asgarian et al.

[86], these studies found the presence of biases resulting in reduced landmark localization accuracy in clinical populations compar ed to healthy age -matched controls [82]–[85]. As a result, a series of studies addressed the need to reduce such biases via fine-tuning the FAN on data from specific patient groups. Bandini et al. released a publicly available dataset , the Toronto NeuroF ace Dataset, from which more than 3,300 manually annotated images of faces from individuals with ALS, post -stroke, and aged matched controls were used to fine -tune FAN [73]. In follow up studies, Guarin et al. showed that fine-tuning FAN using the clinic al data improve d the overall model’s performance [84] as well as improving the performance of subsequent classifiers using detected landmarks in differentiating clinical groups from healthy controls [87]. Guarin et al. further quantified the amount of clinical data needed to fine -tune and improve the performance of FAN [85]. It was found that fine -tuning the model with as little as 8 annotated images from a single patient resulted in better performance on other individuals from the same clinical group. The best performance was achieved by fine tuning the model with 320 image s from 40 patients and using more images from additional patients did not improve the performance further. Overall, the findings show ed that fine -tuning FAN using a relatively small sample of clinically represented data improve d the performance of the mode l as well as the subsequent clinical facial analysis task.

Validation Framework There have been frameworks developed specifically for the digital health technology development and evaluation [88]. The V3 framework describes three phases for the evaluati on of digital biomarkers , namely, verification, analytical validation, and clinical validation [89]. According to this framework, verification refers to validating the hardware and sensors involved in recording the digital measurement. Analytical validat ion refers to the process of ensuring that the digital biomarker measurements are accurately measuring the intended phenomena. Clinical validation is concerned with evaluating whether the digital biomarker provides meaningful clinical information [89]. 10 Robin et al. specifically examined this framework and provided further recommendations of how to use this framework for speech related biomarkers and speech -based measures [90]. Using this framework, the verification step involved comparing various tech nologies and establishing the adequacy of the final technical instrumentation at collecting parameters that can be acceptable digital biomarkers [90]. The verification step of any technology is usually performed by the manufacturer via bench top testing [89]. Analytical step varies depending on the relevant features or methods used to compute the biomarker. This method can entail comparing the new digital biomarker to the existing technical gold standard s, obtaining concurrent validation through compari son with other sensors, or through other methods such as cross -validation and independent replication of results .

At this phase it is also necessary to analyze and eliminat e potential biases in the data as a result of the confounding factors. Clinical val idation is the culmination of technology evaluation. At this stage, the technology outputs are compared with clinical gold standards that have been established to detect disease and track its progression. In the assessment of various neurological disorders , some diseases have a clear gold standard while for many others do not. Therefore, in clinical validation, the choice of reference measure limits the clinical validation as the digital biomarker can only be as good as the measure used to validate it. Clin ical validation can be especially challenging , when the used reference measure has limited validity, or lower reliability, resulting in difficulties achieving consistency [90]. Using this framework, engineers, scientists, and clinicians must work togethe r to bring a technology from a design and prototype phase in to a clinically utilizable tool [90]. Current Technology: Verification The proposed system used in this thesis consists of Intel RealSense 3D Camera to collect data from patients performing standard orofacial tasks as well as use of the FAN model to automatically extract facial landmarks that are then used to further extract facial kinematics . The performance of both components (the Intel RealSense 3D Camera and FAN model) has been previously studie d and verified by their developers as well as the research community. Keselman et al.

[91] performed a comprehensive study on th e Intel RealSense 3D camera and verified the performance of the system under different test conditions. Performance of FAN was tested on 11 some of available open -source datasets and its performance was reported relative to the other facial landmark detection methods , as well as relative to manually annotated facial landmarks [80]. Together the system made from combining these two technologies has the potential to provide clinicians with an objective way of analyzing facial kinematics. However, this system n eeds to analytically and clinically validated before its adoption as a clinical tool, which are main goals/objectives of this thesis. Current Technology: Analytical Validation The analytical validation process evaluates the generated physiological and/or behavioural metrics in experiments involving human subjects [89]. The Intel RealSense 3D Camera has been previously analytically validated in a study where , Ouni et al. [92] compared Intel RealSense 3D against the sensor -based Electromagnetic Articulog raphy (EMA) and found the tracking error of the lower lip to be low and the system suitable for facial applications that requires facial motion tracking. In another study, Bandini et al. [93] compared the Intel RealSense 3D Camera along with Intraface fa cial landmark detection and tracking algorithm against the marker -based optical tracking system and found the lips Root Mean Square Error ( RMSE ) to be between 1 -3 mm range. Harkel et al.

[94] have conducted a reliability study comparing Intel RealSense 3D Camera against a highly costly and accurate 3D facial tracking system and found high agreement between the two methods in patients with unilateral facial palsy. The accurate tracking of the f acial landmarks is a prerequisite to performing clinically meaningful facial analysis. The development of new and more accurate architectures remains an active area of research and their adoption in developing objective clinical tools is on the rise [95]. However, according to the V3 framework, new analytical validations are needed when there are changes/upgrades to either the software and/or hardware [89]. Thus, as there were no analytical validation studies done previously that uses the Intel RealSens e 3D Camera together with the FAN to analyze facial kinematics, the first study of this thesis aims at evaluating this technology. Current Technology: Clinical Validation Clinical validation process evaluates whether the proposed technology provides any valuable clinical insight in a specific context [89]. Clinically, the primary goals of an assessment are to detect clinical features that point to the presence of an abnormality that can lead to disease 12 diagnosis and/ or allows tracking the disease progre ssion over time. Clinical gold standards carry these two important missions – detection/ diagnosis vs. disease tracking [96], [97] . The proposed technology has been previously validated in automatic assessment of facial video recordings during speech and non-speech tasks to distinguishing healthy vs.

post -stroke [39], healthy vs. Parkinson’s disease [41], and healthy vs ALS individuals [23]. Several facial landmark detection and tracking algorithms have also been clinically validated in other applications such as: automatic pain detection from facial images or videos of participants [98], [99] , automatic detection of patients with neurologic al disorders (e.g. ALS, stroke, PD, and AD) from healthy participants using facial kinematics [39], [42], [100] –[103] , and automatic detection of psychiatric conditions (e.g. depression, and schizophrenia) [77], [104], [105] . In all these studies, the main goal was to extract clinically interpretable objective facial features from the landmarks such as facial dynamics and kinematic measures and then to use these measures to detect the presence of disease via classification methods . Another important go al of these measures is to track disease progression over time. For that, correlations with clinical gold standards must be established. The studies predicting the severity of symptoms have been limited, but important in the effort to clinically validate n ovel assessment technologies. One study showed that kinematics of facial expressions was able to predict the depression severity estimated via the Beck Depression Inventory -II (BDI -II) [105] . The goal of this thesis is to further clinically validate the use of specific set of deep learning algorithm (FAN and fine -tuned FAN) together with the Intel RealSense 3D camera for use in the clinical setting for detecting severity of orofacial impairments within clinical groups.

Summary: Motivation and Gap Detec tion of most neurodegenerative diseases are highly dependent to the clinicians’ experience and are not well standardized [15]. This may lead to delayed diagnosis, and therefore, delayed interventions with reduced possibilities to slow down the rapid reduction of quality of life for the patients. Recent advances in computational power and deep learning algorithms have provided an opportunity to develop potential solutions for automated assessment and early detection of neuro logical disorders in clinic and remotely, in home settings . 13 Previous studies have established the Intel RealSense 3D camera’s technical verification and show n its high accuracy and performance on bench top experiments [34], [91] . Some studies have also shown analytical and clinical validation of the Intel RealSense 3D camera [29], [43], [73], [94] . Other studies have used facial landmark algorithms to perf orm clinically relevant facial analysis [41], [42], [73], [106] . However, the combination of the Intel RealSense 3D camera with FAN to perform objective orofacial assessments in clinical groups with facial impairments have not been fully evaluated in a c ontext of V3 framework before . This research thesis focuses on the analytical and clinical validat ion of the proposed technology (the Intel RealSense 3D camera and FAN) with the aim of advancing its translation into clinical settings.

Research Question an d Objectives The ultimate objective of this research is to implement an affordable, clinically valid, easy to-use video -based application to support the detection and assessment of orofacial deficits in neurological diseases in order to support clinicians with the disease diagnosis and prognosis. The objective of this thesis is to analytically and clinically validate the use of an accessible 3D camera and a machine learning -based facial landmark tracking method for extracting kinematic facial features that are representative of the perceptual clinical scores provided by trained clinicians to individuals with orofacial impairments due to neurological disorders (e.g., post -stroke and ALS). In study 1, analytical validation was determined by comparing the kinematic signals extracted from the proposed system (the Intel RealSense 3D camera and FAN) against the gold standard technology – a sensor -based system EMA . In study 2, c linical validity was determined using correlations between the objective kinematic features and perceptual clinical assessments performed by an expert clinician . In the first stu dy (Chapter 2) regarding analytical validation, we hypothesize that the newly proposed system would perform as well as the accepted gold standard method (EMA) with high correlation between the two methods.

In the second study ( Chapter 3) about clinical val idation, w e hypothesized that the kinematic features and corresponding perceptual clinical scores – symmetry, range of motion (ROM), and speed – would be significantly correlated with corresponding clinical scores and could be used to predict the severity of oromotor impairment. 14 Structure of This Document This thesis is structured as follows: Chapter 1 provides a general introduction to the research topic as well as the rationale for this study. It provides an overview of the related literature for the t echnologies used in this thesis for performing objective orofacial assessments of neurological disorders, including, 3D cameras, AI based facial detection and tracking algorithms , and application of such algorithms in detection of orofacial impairments. It also lays out the problem, the motivation behind the research, the research framework and objective s, and contributions of this work. Chapter 2 is the manuscript describing the analytical validation of video -based technology for assessing orofacial movements Chapter 3 is the manuscript describing the clinical validation of these technologies in assessing orofacial impairments in clinical groups Chapter 4 provides an in -depth discussion of the obtained results , study limitations as well as outlines potential future directions for th is research.
