University of Toronto, Toronto, 2022 [Submitted to the Journal of Speech, Language, and Hearing Research] 15 Chapter 2 – Analytical Validation 2.0 Abstract Facial movements provide valuable information for the assessment of orofacial functions such as speech, swallowing, and facial expressions. Due to the high cost and cumbersome setup procedures, sensor -based technologies have not been adopted in the clinical settings. With the recent advancements in markerless facial tracking algorithms as well as developments of 3D cameras, it has become possible to develop viable clinical applications for the diagnosis and treatment of motor spee ch disorders. In this study, we assessed the performance of a video -based system composed of a deep neural net facial tracking algorithm coupled with Intel RealSense camera to track the lip movements. The validation was performed on three healthy control participants performing a set of speech and non -speech tasks similar to a routine orofacial assessment conducted in clinical settings. Preliminary results show moderate to strong correlation between this method and the gold standard sensor based electromagn etic articulography.

Further algorithm training and tests are needed in order to improve the performance of such system in order to develop an accurate clinical tool for the assessment of orofacial movements. 2.1 Introduction Accurate assessment of orofacial movements is important for the diagnosis and treatment of various neurological disorders. Changes in the movement of the lips and the jaw have been associated with early signs of oromotor decline, disease progression, and speech intelligibility decli ne in neurological diseases such as amyotrophic lateral sclerosis (ALS), Parkinson’s disease (PD), and stroke [107], [108] . Sensor -based technologies such as electromagnetic articulography (EMA) and optical tracking systems are well established methods f or the quantitative assessment of orofacial movements. However, such technologies are not clinically feasible due to their high cost, system complexity, and long and tedious setup procedures. As a result, the quantitative analysis of orofacial movement has been limited to research purposes and not integrated into clinical practice in neurology or speech -language pathology [23], [40], [92], [109] . There is a need for an inexpensive, easy -to-use, and clinically feasible orofacial movement tracking system th at is able to accurately and quickly quantify patient performance in a clinical setting.

Recent advances in computational power and deep learning algorithms coupled with the development of 3D depth cameras have provided an opportunity to develop affordable , markerless, automated, and clinically objective solutions for the assessment of neurological disorders affecting orofacial functions [41], [93], [110], [111] . 16 The evaluation of 3D cameras as potential tools for facial tracking has begun by the researc h community. Ouni and Dahmani assessed the performance and precision of two 3D markerless systems (Primesense and Intel RealSense cameras) with respect to an optoelectronic marker -based system in sentence and syllable productions. They used the commercial software Faceshift to track the virtual markers manually placed on the 3D mesh of the facial model in a post-processing step. The outcome of the study proved overall good performance and low RMSE for both markerless systems, with Intel RealSense 3D depth c amera being slightly more accurate than the Primesense (RMSE < 2 mm) [92]. Bandini et al. evaluated the accuracy of Primesense depth sensor alongside InteraFace facial landmark tracking software package [112] for 3D tracking of lip movements in various speech tasks with RMSE range of 1 -4 mm [93]. In a later study, Bandini et al. were able to discriminate people with PD from healthy control participants using lower lip movement during syllable repetitions und er the same video -based settings and confirmed that patients have reduced articulatory kinematics [41]. Bandini et al.

also validated the video based tracking of jaw movements with Intel RealSense SR300 camera and the RealSense SDK R3 face tracking algor ithm against the EMA during speech with the similar accuracy (average RMSE ~ 2 mm) [40]. Other preliminary work ha s focused on and shown high promise for utilizing open source video -based facial landmark tracking algorithms in clinical assessments of ind ividuals with orofacial deficits, demonstrating high accuracy (up to 87%) in detecting orofacial impairments [110], [111] . As such, the role of computer vision research to study human face for clinical applications has thrived over the past few years, wi th many applications in neurological diseases [41], [98], [110], [111] . Despite a significant promise to revolutionize neurological assessment, further research is needed to validate the use of deep learning facial landmark tracking models in assessing orofacial movements. Markerless methods use various computer vision algorithms and machine learning models to retrieve and track facial landmarks in video data. As a result, the quality of the tracking is highly dependent on the tracking model used [92]. The goal of this study was to evaluate the feasibility of state -of-the-art markerless video -based system, as compared to the gold standard EMA for tracking lip movements. The markerless system consisted of a pre trained convolutional neural network (CNN) m odel for facial landmark tracking as well as a 3D depth camera registered with the video camera in order to translate 2D facial landmarks tracked by the CNN model into 3D locations.

17 2.2 Method 2.2.1 Experimental Setup and Procedure Three healthy young female volunteers were recruited for the experiment. The demographic details of participants is provided on Table A -1 in Appendix A1. The participants were recorded via 3D (video and depth ) camera as well as EMA system while performed a series of speech and non -speech tasks typically used by clinicians in a routine assessment of orofacial and speech function. The tasks w ere expanded to include conditions (e.g., fast, slow) which are known to enhance performance variability within a speaker. The tasks and conditions were: Sentence repetition: The sentence “Buy Bobby a Puppy” was repeated five times at normal and slow spea king rates, both with normal loudness. Max opening: The mouth was opened to the maximum comfortable range and then closed to the rest position, five times. Four variations of the task were performed, namely, (1) Hold: where participants held the maximum o pening for five seconds, (2) Normal: maximum opening followed by closing at a normal speed, (3) Fast: the task was performed as fast as possible, and (4) Asymmetric: opened the mouth asymmetrically to one side (left or right). Max spread: The lips were sp read out to the sides without opening, starting from the rest position without a pause (Normal), or with five seconds pause (Hold) at the maximum spread, repeated five times. The recordings were performed in a quiet room, with a single constant and unifor m light source placed behind the camera. Participants were seated during the experiment looking straight at the camera.

Head movements were monitored and large head movements during experimental recordings were avoided. The EMA sensors were placed using cl ear surgical tape on the participants’ face prior to data acquisition, and the camera was adjusted to be at each participant’s eye level. The Wave electromagnetic generator was located on the right side of participants’ head, while the 3D camera was placed at a distance of 0.3 -0.4 m away from the tip of the nose. 18 2.2.2 Data Acquisition 1) The Wave speech research system (NDI – Waterloo, Ontario, Canada) was used to track lip movements. The Wave system consists of an electromagnetic field generator and sens ors that report 5 degrees -of-freedom position information within the set boundaries of the generated EM field with high tracking accuracy (average error = 0.8 mm), thus, making it the “gold standard” for validation of video -based systems [109] . Eight sen sors were attached to the participants’ faces in the following positions: N1: placed at the intersection of the frontal and nasal bone N2: placed at the tip of the nose . LU (LB): placed at the upper and the vermilion of the upper and lower lips, respective ly. LR (LL): placed at the right and left corners of the mouth, respectively . JR (JL): placed on the inferior of the jaw about one quarter of the distance from the gnathion and the right (left) gonion. The 3D position of the sensors over time (trajectorie s) were collected at the sampling frequency of 100 Hz. Data were recorded and stored using the Wavefront software v.1.1.

2) The video -based markerless system consists of an Intel RealSense SR300 (Intel Corp., Santa Clara, CA, USA) camera that acquires registered color and depth video recordings, as well as the FAN model [80]. FAN is an open -source CNN model used to automatically detect the face, local ize 68 facial landmarks, and track them throughout the duration of the videos. The videos were collected at the resolution of 640×480 pixels at 30 frames per second (fps) rate with color (RGB) and depth recording occurring simultaneously. Videos were reco rded using custom C++ code. 19 2.2.3 Data Analysis 1) Pre -processing: A custom Python script was developed to process and analyze data obtained from each of the two data recording methods used. Because both EMA sensor recordings and the video recordings ran domly drop a few data points resulting in inconsistencies within the sampling rate, both data streams were resampled at a uniform rate of 100 Hz using linear interpolation. A low -pass Butterworth filter (8 -poles, with cut -off frequency of 15 Hz) was used on the trajectories of the points of interests (landmarks and sensor trajectories) to eliminate jitter. 2) Kinematic measures: As interpretable decisions are crucial in clinical applications, the measures of range of motion and average speed, aligned with typical clinical judgements, were selected for the comparisons. The range of motion (ROM) was estimated vertically and horizontally.

The vertical range of motion (VRM) was calculated as the Euclidean distance (in mm) between LU and LB (Wave) and the dist ance between landmarks 52 and 58 Figure 2 -1. The horizontal range of motion (HRM) was calculated as the Euclidean distance between lip corners - RC and LC (Wave) and the distance between landmarks 49 and 55 (refer to Figure 2 -1). The velocity signals (in mm/s) were computed as the first derivative of the calculated displacements with respect to time. 3) Validation: The phase correlation in Fourier space [113] was used to align the signals obtained from each method for all the recordings. The zero -lag cross correlation (CC0) was computed to measure the agreement between the two signals obtained from each of the methods for both displacement and velocity features extracted . These measures were calculated for each task and then averaged across the three participants. The tasks were then par sed into individual repetitions and the maximum value of ROM and average speed were calculated for each repetition of the two methods used. The Spearman’s correlation (SC) coefficient was then calculated for each task across all the repetitions for each participant and across the three participants. 20 Figure 2 -1 Position of EM sensors (purple dots) and facial landmarks (blue asterisk s) 2.3 Results Figure 2-2 shows an example of VRM of the mouth during the max opening and sentence repetition tasks followed by HRM during the max spread task. 24 2.4 Discussion This preliminary study showed high correlations between the EMA and video -based tracking systems with zero -lag cross -correlation values > 0.90 for non -speech tasks and >0.81 for the speech repetition task, when looking at the entire signal s. However, the correlation values were smaller when extracting the maximum ROM and average speed values on signals parsed by repetition to obtain discrete, clinically relevant measures. This could potentially be due to the jitter in the facial landmarks o btained using FAN [80], particularly when calculating the correlation between velocity values. In future studies, additional signal processing or improved facial landmarks tracking will need to be considered to reduce frame -to-frame jitter. The correlati on values were higher across participant s as opposed to within each participant ’s repetition s of the task. This is due to the fact that the variability of kinematic values were low within each participants ’ repetitions compared to the trend across all the participant ’s data. As illustrated in Figure 2-2, there was an expected bias in the signals obtained from the two m ethods.

This bias was due to the fact that the two methods measured different distances: the ground truth signal (blue) was the distance between two sensor locations, while the FAN tracking signal (red) was the distance between two automatically detected f acial landmarks (lip edges). The positions of these reference points w ere naturally not the same. In addition, some discrepancies between the signals are likely to be due to the sampling rate difference between the two methods, as the video were recorded a t a lower sampling rate than the EMA data (30 fps compared to 100 Hz). Future work should aim to record data at the minimum sampling rate of 50 Hz, as recommended by Ouni and Dahmani [92], to enable fine modeling of lip movements. Figure 2-3 shows that the video -based system was able to successfully differentiate between different conditions within individuals (i.e., as expected, t he fast condition showed smaller ROM and greater average speed) for the maximum open task. Additionally, it was able to differentiate between speakers, as some participants naturally use larger or smaller movements than other participants. This differentia tion is clinically relevant as clinicians are tasked to visually identify the movement ROM and speed. These perceptual judgements have been known to have reduced reliability, and the potential use of video -based methods to distinguish individual difference s is highly promising. 25 Clear advantages of the video -based tracking ha ve emerged.

First, the acquisition volume for the EMA – determined by the Wave field generator placed near the participant’s head – was smaller than the adjustable and larger volume of video -based tracking. In EMA, even small head movements away from the generator’s field resulted in the loss of tracking by the sensors. We had to closely monitor the hea d position of the participants in the EMA field. However, such small movements were a ccounted for in the video tracking via automated face detection and the detection of facial landmarks so long as the face remained within view of the camera, thus, avoiding the complete loss of data. Furthermore, electromagnetic tracking was limited by the number of sensors placed on the face and highly reduced/limited movement information available for EMA, while the current state -of-the-art facial tracking algorithm tracked large number of facial features (e.g. 68 landmarks). Although we extracted relativ ely small number of highly straightforward features, the work on the most important feature is still ongoing [42]. In conclusion, this study has demonstrated that the video -based tracking using the FAN [80] has a great potential in tracking facial fea tures relative to the gold standards. Further improvements to its ability to detect facial landmarks needs to establish across a large number of participants with and without neurological disease and across tasks. This ongoing work can help in accurate tra cking of facial movements, thus, providing clinicians with guidance in the assessment of neurological disorders.

26 Chapter 3 – Clinical Validation Study 3.0 Abstract This study sought to determine whether clinically interpretable kinematic features extracted automatically from 3 dimensional (3D) videos were correlated with corresponding perceptual clinical orofacial ratings in individuals with orofacial impairments due to neurological disorders. 45 participants (19 diagnosed with motor neuron diseases (MND), and 26 post -stroke) performed two non -speech tasks (mouth opening and lip spreading) and one speech task (repetition of a sentence “Buy Bobby a Puppy”) while being video recorded in a standardized lab setting. The colour video recordings of participants were assessed by an expert clinician – a speech language pathologist - on the severity of three orofacial measures: symmetry, range of motion (ROM), and speed. Clinic ally interpretable 3D kinematic features, linked to symmetry, ROM and speed, were automatically extracted from video recordings, using a deep facial landmark detection and tracking algorithm, for each of the three tasks. Spearman correlations were used to identify features that were significantly correlated (p -value < .05) with their corresponding clinical scores. Clinically significant kinematic features were then used in the subsequent multivariate regression models to predict the overall orofacial impai rment severity score. Several kinematic features extracted from 3D video recordings were associated with their corresponding perceptual clinical scores, indicating clinical validity of these automatically derived measures.

Different patterns of significant features were observed between MND and post -stroke groups; these differences were aligned with clinical expectations in both cases. The results show that kinematic features extracted automatically from simple clinical tasks can capture characteristics use d by clinicians during assessments. These findings support the clinical validity of video -based automatic extraction of kinematic features. 3.1 Introduction Neurological disorders often affect orofacial and speech functions [6]. For example, people post-stroke or living with motor neuron diseases (MND) often experience orofacial motor impairments (i.e., inability to produce facial expressions or perform volitional orofacial movements), as well as dysarthria, a motor speech disorder [114] , [115] . Accurate assessment of orofacial speech and non -speech movements becomes important for effective disease detection, tracking progression, and monitoring treatment outcomes [15]. Existing orofacial assessment methods rely on either subjective clinical evaluation performed by experts or on the use of complex and expensive sensor -based techniques, such as electromagnetic articulography (EMA), that are not suitable for integration int o current clinical workflows. Emerging video -based methods may offer easy -to-use and cost -effective solutions to 27 support objective clinical assessment of orofacial motor impairments.

The development of 3D video cameras and deep learning algorithms has enab led accurate automatic detection and tracking of pre -defined facial landmarks in facial images [80], which is the first essential step for many clinical facial analysis applications. In order to be adopted into a clinical setting, novel facial tracking t echnologies must be evaluated within established frameworks for digital biomarker development, such as the FDA BEST (FDA, 2021) criteria and the V3 framework [89]. The V3 framework advocates for a 3 -part sequential biomarker evaluation procedure: (1) ver ification of sensor -level outputs, typically conducted by manufacturers, followed by (2) analytical validation of a novel method with respect to an already -validated technical gold standards, followed by (3) clinical validation to assess utility in a speci fic clinical population and relative to existing clinical standards/ practices. The technical verification of the facial tracking technologies has been previously well established [29], [34], [91]. The analytical validation of markerless methods has demo nstrated promising results as well. Ouni and Dahmani found that facial tracking methods using two different 3D cameras, namely, Intel RealSense (Intel Corporation, Santa Clara, California, USA) and PrimeSense Carmine (PrimeSense, Tel Aviv, Israel) had good accuracy with respect to optoelectronic tracking, which functioned as a sensor -based ground truth, specifically the Vicon system (Vicon Motion Systems Ltd, Oxford, GB) [92]. Bandini et al.

performed an analogous comparison between a 3D camera (Intel Rea lSense) and an EMA system (NDI Wave Speech Research System – Waterloo, Ontario, Canada), and demonstrated good accuracy of the camera compared to the Wave [40]. Harkel et al. evaluated the intra - and inter -rater reliability and agreement of the Intel Rea lSense D415 camera against a gold standard stereophotogrammetry imaging system, 3dMD, in patients with facial palsy and found that the 3D camera was a viable option to be implemented in the context of an objective clinical assessment [94]. Taken together , these previous studies have extensively demonstrated the analytical validity of 3D camera -based facial tracking methods. Beyond verification and analytical validation, the technology (both 3D camera and deep learning algorithms) must be evaluated in cli nical populations in order to fulfill the third step of biomarker development. Previous work has shown promise for detecting orofacial impairments associated with stroke [116] , Parkinson’s disease [41], [101] , and amyotrophic lateral sclerosis (ALS) [42] relative to neurologically healthy controls, based on measures of facial kinematics 28 (e.g., velocity, acceleration, range of motion, symmetry, etc.). For example, Bandini et al used a small feature set capturing movement symmetry, speed, and range of m otion (ROM) of the lips and jaw to automatically differentiate between post stroke patients and controls with 87% accuracy [116] .

In another study, a small number of 3D kinematic features of lip movement were used to discriminate patients with ALS from h ealthy subjects with nearly 90% accuracy [42]. Neumann et al. used a combination of kinematic features extracted from 2D video recordings together with acoustic features extracted from audio data to distinguish patients with ALS at symptomatic versus pre-symptomatic phases of bulbar disease as well as from the control participants [117] . The focus of previous studies was to investigate video -based methods’ use for classifying a group of participants into either the healthy or clinical category. The st udies predicting the severity of symptoms have been limited, but important in the effort to clinically validate novel assessment technologies. One study showed that kinematics of facial expressions was able to predict the depression severity estimated via the Beck Depression Inventory -II (BDI -II) [105] . In another study, features were extracted from facial expression images of participants in order to automatically differentiate among five levels of pain using AI -based models evaluated its efficacy on two open -source datasets [118] . Additionally, the Unified Parkinson’s Disease Rating Scale (UPDRS) motor score was predicted using acoustic features extracted from 5875 voice recordings of 42 patients with PD with the final ensemble model predicting the cli nical score with 99.6% accuracy [119] .

These promising results highlighted the need for more research to clinically validate automated facial analysis technologies for their use as assessment tools, specifically for predicting the clinical scores associated with severity of orofacial impairme nts. The ability to predict disease severity using kinematic measures extracted from videos would provide evidence of the clinical validity of the technology and support its use to enable tracking disease progression as well as evaluating the effectiveness of intervention programs (e.g., quantifying functional improvement post -stroke). The first objective of this study was to establish clinical validity of a video -based system in assessing the severity of the orofacial impairments by automatically extracti ng kinematic features from video recordings of individuals with orofacial impairments either due to stroke or MND and comparing them with respect to their corresponding clinical scores (e.g., clinical estimates of symmetry/speed/ROM and kinematic measures of symmetry/speed/ROM). The 29 second objective was to determine whether a combination of kinematic features was able to predict the overall severity of orofacial deficit in each patient group. We hypothesized that the selected kinematic features and correspo nding perceptual clinical scores would be significantly correlated and could be used to predict the overall severity of orofacial impairment.

3.2 Methods 3.2.1 Participants Data from 45 patient participants were included in this study: 19 individuals were diagnosed with motor neuron disease (MND, including ALS= 16; primary lateral sclerosis= 1; Kennedy’s disease= 2), and 26 individuals who experienced a stroke (PS). We combined all MND patients into the same group as the manifestation of the disease in the orofacial mechanism was expected to be similar (e.g., resulting primarily in kinematic changes due to primarily muscle weakness). Table 3-1 presents the demographic and clinical representation of the samples. The more in-depth clinical details of particip ants are provided in Table A -2 and Table A -3 in Appendix A2. All participants were cognitively unimpaired (Montreal Cognitive Assessment - MoCA score ≥ 26 [120] and passed a hearing screening at 25 dB for both ears at 500, 1000, 2000, and 4,000 Hz. The MND group was also evaluated via ALS – Functional Rating Scale – Revised (ALSFRS R) [121] . This measure is routinely used by clinicians to evaluate bulbar dysfunction and consists of only 3 questions to address changes in speech, swallowing, and sa livation that is rated on a four point ordinal scale with 0 indicating no function and 4 indicating normal function [122] . The study was approved by the Research Ethics boards at the Sunnybrook Research Institute and University Health Network: Toronto Rehabilitation Institute, Toronto, Canada. All participants provided informed consent according to the requirements of the Declaration of Helsinki.

The rater only knew the diagnosis of the participants prior to performing the assessment and otherwise was blind to all other clinical information (e.g., scores on other clinical assessments, site of onset, disease duration, etc.). Ratings were based on a 5 -point Likert scale with 1 indicating normal function and 5 indicating severe dysfunction. ROM, speed, and symmetry scores were used as separate clinical outcomes; they were selected as they constitute salient neuromuscular abnormalities in motor speech disorders [123] . The total score – a sum of all 5 clinical ratings – was calculated and used to represent the overall orofacial deficit severity, with score of 5 indicating normal function and score of 25 i ndicating the most severe dysfunction (see [73]).

To determine the reliability of the clinician scores, the original examinator (intra -rater reliability) and another trained examinator (a certified SLP; interrater reliability) re -scored videos from 15% of the sample (7 participants chosen randomly; 21 recordings). To assess intra -rater reliability, Intraclass Correlation Coefficient (ICC) estimates and their 95% confidence intervals (CI) were calculated using SPSS statistical package version 23 (SPSS Inc , Chicago, IL) based on a single -rating, absolute -agreement, 2 -way mixed -effects model [124] . To assess inter -rater agreement, ICC estimates and their 95% CI were calculated based on a single -rating, consistency agreement, 2 -way random -effects model [124]. A percentage of clinical agreement (percentage of scores within 1 -point difference) was also calculated to mitigate the effects of skewed distributions or low variability of the scores in ICC values, if necessary. The intra -rater agreement was strong (ICC = 0.966, CI = 0.908 – 0.987, p < .001; [125] ). The inter -rater agreement was also excellent (ICC = 0.996, CI = [phone-redacted], p < .001; [125] ). All scores fell within 1 -point difference for both intra-rater and inter -rater reliability. 3.2.3 Data collection procedures Video recordings were obtained while participants were seated in front of an Intel RealSense depth camera (SR300 or D400) at approximately 30 -60 cm distance away from the face 31 at the eye level height. The recordings were perform ed in a quiet room with a single source uniform light illuminating the participants’ face.

Each video recording was composed of a pair of color (RGB) and depth videos. Both streams were recorded simultaneously at approximately 50 frames per second at VGA r esolution of 640×480 pixels. Participants were asked to perform a set of standard speech and non -speech tasks commonly used by clinicians during orofacial and motor speech examinations to assess the severity of orofacial symptoms and dysarthria. The subj ects were asked to avoid head and body movements and look directly into the camera during recordings. The following set of tasks were chosen for analysis as they captured the orofacial movements of mouth and jaw in both horizontal and vertical directions a nd were affected in both clinical populations in our previous studies [23], [39]: • 10 repetitions of the sentence “Buy Bobby a Puppy” at a comfortable speaking rate and loudness (BBP) • 5 repetitions of maximum jaw opening and closing (OPEN) • 5 repetitions of pretending to smile with tight lips (SPREAD) Furthermore, we recorded 10 seconds of participants resting, while they maintained a neutral facial expression (REST) with their teeth together but not clenched. This was done for distance normalization purpo ses. A total of 175 video recordings were included in the final analysis: 74 from MND, and 101 from PS. A total of 5 recordings were either missing or excluded.

Reasons for exclusion of a video recording for a task were: participant not performing the tas k (2 cases – in MND group, BBP task), video recording issues, e.g., corruption of either the colour and/or depth stream data of video recordings, or dropping of video frames (2 case – in PS group, BBP task), and failed accurate facial landmark tracking due to poor lighting condition (1 case – in PS group, BBP task). Each of the remaining videos, excluding the data for the REST task, were manually segmented into individual repetitions by a trained research assistant (i.e., 5 segments for the non -speech tasks and 10 segments for the speech task). 32 3.2.4 Kinematic Data Analysis Preprocessing A fine -tuned version of the FAN [80] was used to extract 68 facial landmarks from colour videos in 2 dimensions (2D) pixel values and track them throughout the duration of the videos (see Figure 3-1). The 3D positions of landmarks were then calculated by aligning and sampling the depth images at the same pixel coordinates as the landmark points identified on the color images. The 3D real world coordinates of the landmark s (in meters) were calculated using the intrinsic camera parameters together with aligning the depth and colour streams, thus, obtaining 3D coordinates for each landmark, with X representing the lateral axis, Y representing the vertical axis, and Z represe nting the frontal axis. Figure 3-1 Facial landmarks obtained from the fine-tuned FAN. The landmarks used for extracting kinematic features are named and highlighted in light blue dots.

The red dashed lines are the kinematic displacement measures extracted from specific facial landmark pairs. It has been previously shown that the state -of-the-art deep neural facial landmark detection models do not perform well when used on faces of older adults or patients with orof acial deficits [73], [83] . To address this issue, the model was previously fine -tuned using 4340 images extracted from video recordings of individuals from clinical populations as well as healthy older adults, some of which were of a subset of the indivi duals in the present study, performing orofacial tasks [126] . The normalized Root Mean Squared Error (NRMSE) of the fine -tuned model was reported

33 to be 4.7 ± 0.7 %, 6.2 ± 1.7 %, and 5.7 ± 1.1 % for healthy control, post -stroke group, and ALS group, respectively [126] . The fine -tuned FAN model has been previously used to successfully detect oromotor impairments associated with various neurological disorders [73], [83] . To check the mode l’s accuracy, a video file was generated of participants performing each task with the location of 2D landmarks superimposed on their faces. Each video was viewed to ensure adequate detection and tracking of facial landmarks of interest. Out of 68 facial landmarks, 10 landmarks from lips and jaw were considered in this study. They included: Forehead (FH), left medial canthus of eye (LE), right medial canthus of eyes (RE), upper vermillion lip border at mouth centre (UL), lower vermillion lip border (LL) at mouth centre, left oral commissure (LC), right oral commissure (RC), centre jawline (CJ), left jawline (LJ), and right jawline (RJ). To remove tracking noise and jitter, the trajectories of the landmarks of the interests were lowpass filtered with an 8 -pole Butterworth filter with cut -off frequency of 12 Hz [73], [127] . Kinematic Features Similar to previous studies, facial landmarks were used to derive clinically interpretable and relevant kinematic features [42], [84] . These features are summarized in Table 3-2.

Features were selected to correspond to clinical judgements of ROM, speed and symmetry by the speech language pathologist. A total of 20 kinematic features were linked to ROM, 24 to speed, and 9 to symmetry. All the features have been used i n previous studies [23], [101] . ROM Features The 3D Euclidean distances between a set of facial landmarks were calculated to establish lip opening, UL – LL; lip spread, RC – LC; movement of LL with respect to the FH reference point; movement of RC with respect to RE; and movement of LC with respect to LE. In addition, the total movement of the mouth was modelled by a sum of the areas made by two triangles defined by lip landmarks {RC, UL, LL} and {LC, UL, LL}. Lip spread as well as other RC/LC measures were established only for SPREAD, where horizontal movements were predominant. Since the jaw rarely moves during SPREAD, none of the jaw -based features were considered for this task. All the calculated distance features were normalized with respect to the corresponding values 34 calculated in the REST condition. After this normalization, ROM was determined as the maximum value of each of these distances/areas, as well as the cumulative sum path length. This was done for each repetition. Speed Features The fir st derivative of the above path distances with respect to time were calculated to represent the velocity of the lips and jaw movements. The maximum, minimum, and average values were considered.

Symmetry Features The symmetry of movement was characterized by (1) comparing the motion of the left and right corners of the mouth with respect to the reference points LE and RE and (2) comparing the areas on the right and left triangles defined by {RC, UL, LL} and {LC, UL , LL}, respectively. The absolute differences, ratios, and concordance correlation coefficient (CCC [128] ; as a measure of agreement of two signals) between the computed values of the distances and mouth areas on the right and left sides were calculated. Smaller CCC values corresponded to asymmetry during facial movements. Similarly, the larger deviation of right/left ratios away from 1 indicated more asymmetrical facial movements.

Prior to further analyses, outliers were removed from the data using the inter -quartile range (IQR) method [129] . In this method, the IQR of the repetitions were calculated, then multiplied by 2.5, and added/subtracted from the third/first quartiles. The values per repetition of each task that fell outside this range were considered outliers and were eliminated from the set. In total, 4.7% of the repetitions were discarded as outliers (n=51). The arithmetic mean of kinematic feature values were calculated cross the remaining repetitions per subject, per task. All subsequent analyses were conducted separately for each task (OPEN, SPREAD, BBP) and group (MND vs. post -stroke). The Spearman correlation coefficients were calculated between each kinematic feature and their corresponding perceptual clinical scores. The Spearman rho ( r) is used with ordinal data and measures the degree of monotonic relationship between two variables; here, in accordance with accepted guidelines, values between 0 to ±0.20 were considered as negligible, ±0.21 to ±0.40 as weak, ±0.41 to ±0.60 as moderate, ±0.61 to ±0.80 as strong, and ±0.81 to ± 1.00 as very strong [130] .

The significant level for each correlation was set to 0.05 to reflect that these correlations were primarily used as a screening method for subsequent regression analyses. 36 Kinematic features with statistically significant corr elations were included in the subsequent multivariable linear regression models with the total orofacial clinical score as the outcome. The Variance Inflation Factor (VIF) with threshold of greater than 10 was used to assess the collinearity between covari ates [131] . If the VIF for the variables were higher than the set threshold, the features with highest VIF were eliminated iteratively until the remaining variables all had VIF values less than 10. The normality of residuals was tested by plotting the Q -Q norm of the residuals. The linearity of fitted regression models was also evaluated by plotting the residual values against the kinematic features data. The adjusted R2, adjusted for the number of predictors, was reported for the final fitted regression models. 3.3 Results 3.3.1 Clinical Scores Figure 3-2 shows the distribution of the severity of the clinical scores for each orofacial task and participant group. Notably, the variation of the clinical scores was task dependent; a larger spread of all sco res was noted for the SPREAD task in both groups and to some extent in BBP, particularly for the MND group. A relatively small, clinically interpr etable lip and jaw kinematic feature set was delineated from the outset; the features were chosen to conceptually represent the measures used by clinicians to assess orofacial deficits during the oromotor examinations of non -speech and speech tasks. The st udy revealed that subsets of kinematic features were significantly correlated with their corresponding clinical scores for each clinical group.

The sets of statistically significant features that were able to explain a significant portion of data variabili ty in the overall severity scores in most models were unique for each of the clinical subgroups. Overall, the findings demonstrated the potential of automated video -based systems for extraction of kinematic features in the context of orofacial neurological assessment and established their initial validity as clinical tools. 3.4.1 Facial kinematics in MND and PS In total, there were 16 statistically significant correlations (out of 50, 32%) across all three tasks for the MND group. In the PS group, 23 individual correlations (8 for SPREAD and 15 for BBP) of kinematic features with clinical scores were significant (46%). Although less than 50% of correlations were significant, and those that were significant ranged between 0.40 and 0.74 (moderate to good), the results were overall encouraging. Smaller correlations and model fits have been expected based on previous literature exploring relationships between kinematics and other measurement domains, including perceptual and acoustic (see Lee et al., 2018; Rong et al., 2012; Teplansky et al., 2022) . However, the findings of this study aligned strongly with those of previous work with regards to facial kinematics and oromotor phenomena in both MND and PS groups. In the MND group, our findings were consistent with previous literature describing abnormal jaw and lip movements [42], [127], [135], [136] .

MND has been characterized by weakness in the orofacial musculature [137] and, expectedly, ROM and speed measures were noted to be substantially more affected by MND than symmetry (see Shellikeri et al. , 2016) . As a result, we observed significant 43 correlations between the clinical scores indicating the impairment severity and kinematic features primarily related to ROM and speed. In the PS group, facial paresis, resulting in facial asymmetry with a un ilateral muscle impairment of lower half of the face, causing asymmetric smile and other facial gestures, is one of the most common impairments following a stroke [116], [139] . As anticipated, the symmetry features played a prominent role in the PS group , and our finding showed significant correlations between kinematically assessed symmetry and the corresponding symmetry clinical scores. In both clinical groups, the agreement between clinical characteristics sought by experts to describe the unique clini cal representation of orofacial deficits and corresponding kinematic features supported the overall impression of clinical validity of the video -based movement assessment. 3.4.2 Task differences Differences also emerged in this study between tasks. In th e MND group, kinematic measures of ROM and speed were linked to the corresponding clinical scores in non -speech OPEN and SPREAD tasks, but not in the speech task -BBP. The findings for non -speech tasks were anticipated based on past research [42]. Both ta sks are classified as maximum performance tasks, yet clinically they serve different purposes.

While OPEN primarily is used to judge the integrity of jaw depressor/elevator muscles, SPREAD is used to judge the integrity of intrinsic facial muscles. Previou sly, kinematic features extracted from lower lip and jaw in SPREAD and OPEN were used to differentiate patients with ALS from healthy control participants with 83% and 72% accuracy, respectively [42]. The findings regarding the speech task were somewhat unexpected, as previous studies had shown BBP to be highly sensitive to the presence of the motor neuron disease [23], [127] . Current results for BBP may be due to the challenges associated with clinical (visuo -perceptual) assessment of speech versus non -speech movements by SLPs. BBP is a relatively fast task and as such it might be more challenging for clinicians to rank its clinical characteristics (ROM, speed, and symmetry) on a 5 -point scale. Further, only 2D colour video recordings (no audio) were vi ewed by the expert for clinical judgement of BBP, which might have resulted in a loss of some information (e.g., rate/slowness) essential for forming the overall clinical impression. Limited sample size and high variability (due to factors such as heteroge neity of disease presentation) may have also contributed to these results. 44 In the PS group, results for the vertical opening (OPEN) contrasted with the horizontal opening (SPREAD) and speech (BBP) task. None of the kinematic features were correlated with clinical scores in OPEN, while sets of features were correlated with the clinical scores in SPREAD and BBP.

The lack of correlation among features and the clinical scores in OPEN may be due to the task being mainly used to assess the integrity of the jaw ( i.e., cranial nerve V) and not facial muscles, which might be differentially affected post -stroke [116] . The SPREAD task showed more notable effect as it intended to reveal the integrity of facial muscles (i.e., cranial nerve VII). Although relatively mi ld, this type of impairment predominated in our PS sample. Facial features during BBP also revealed a link with the clinical symmetry score in these patients. Similar results for SPREAD and BBP with respect to their sensitivity to orofacial impairment post -stroke were reported in previous work (Bandini, Green, Richburg, et al., 2018). Overall, with the exception of BBP task in the MND group, task effects observed clinically and kinematically in this study demonstrated results consistent with previous rese arch and, by extension, supported the potential for clinical validity of the tested assessment method. 3.4.3 Modeling overall orofacial impairment severity A combination of kinematic features was able to predict the overall orofacial impairment severity s core in each clinical group and some tasks. This was important to examine as the overall severity score combined clinical impressions across different specific observations that might be difficult to rank separately and individually. In the MND group, sign ificant models were noted for OPEN and SPREAD.

All but one individual variable (LL_Max) contributed to the overall model in OPEN explaining a substantial degree of variability of the total clinical score. The value of the coefficients generally suggested t hat as the severity increased, the values of the ROM and speed of the lips and jaw were reduced. The SPREAD model was significant, but none of the kinematic variables significantly contributed to explaining the model’s overall variance. This may suggest that the SPREAD was not an ideal task for feature extraction in this group, as previous research suggested that the greatest impairment in MND affects the vertical movement plane of the jaw and lips [136] . In the PS group, in contrast with MND, most of the features were significant in the regression model for SPREAD, with kinematic features explaining over half of the variance in the 45 data. The results of this study are promising as the variability of the kinematic measures within and in between subjects in the study were considerable. The value of the coefficients revealed that as the severity increased, the lip movement became more reduced and asymmetrical. In BBP task, total mouth area and the maximum mouth opening velocity were the two statistically signi ficant features with the model being able to explain substantial amount of the variance in the data. These features align with clinicians’ judgement as they assess the overall mouth movement and speed of movement during a fast task such as BBP.

Overall, th e features related to symmetrical movement of the mouth demonstrated to be of the most importance aligned with the clinical perceptual assessment of this clinical group, further strengthening the validity of this method for future automatic objective asses sment. 3.4.4 Limitations and future directions This work had several limitations. The primary limitation was a relatively small sample size and differences in sample sizes of the two clinical groups, limiting our ability to fully compare group results. An other limiting factor was a relatively small severity range in both clinical samples. We capitalized on the greater variability available in our samples based on specific tasks instead. In addition, the kinematic features extracted were averaged across th e repetitions for each subject per task and thus variability of the facial movements during each repetition of the task were not considered. Future work will extend on the results obtained in the study, focus on gathering data from a larger/ balanced group s of participants with more severe orofacial impairments as well as developing predictive models using timeseries of facial landmarks instead of averaging kinematic feature values across repetitions. This work will be essential to develop and integrate mod ern digital technologies into clinical care as well as outcome measures for clinical trials [88]. The present work had limitations to acknowledge and to address in future studies.

The primary limitation was a relatively small sample size and differences in sample sizes of the two clinical groups, limiting our ability to fully compare group results. Small sample sizes remain an issue for many clinicals studies pre and during the pandemic, which expedited the transition of this type of work into the domain of remote data collections/ assessment, for which the current study results are of a particular importance. Further, the intra - and inter -rater reliability was performed on a small sub -sample (15% of the sample). Although it is relatively standard to eval uate 46 approximately that amount of data to establish reliability, the sample there may have been small, introducing the possibility of perceptual bias. Nevertheless, we observed small confidence intervals in the reliability analyses, which supported the ade quacy of this method for its present purposes (i.e., evaluating the performance of raters at a sample level, rather than at a participant level). Another limiting factor was a relatively small severity range in both clinical samples. We capitalized on the greater variability available in our samples based on specific tasks instead. In addition, the kinematic features extracted were averaged across the repetitions for each subject per task and thus variability of the facial movements during each repetition of the task were not considered.

Future work will extend the results obtained in this study, while focusing on gathering data from a larger/balanced groups of participants with more severe orofacial impairments as well as developing predictive models usi ng timeseries of facial landmarks instead of averaging kinematic feature values across repetitions. This work will be essential to develop and integrate modern digital technologies into clinical care as well as outcome measures for clinical trials [88]. This study laid the foundation for larger scale studies with larger sample size across different cohorts to ensure clinical validity of the proposed technology as well as generalizability of the results. 3.5 Conclusion The result of this study showed tha t the automatic extraction of facial kinematic features from facial video recordings of patients performing standard orofacial assessments is a viable solution for an objective and continuous assessment of facial function. Here we demonstrated encouraging support for the validity of our automated assessment method for detecting the severity of kinematic facial impairments. Development of such systems and their validation with larger datasets holds great promise for the development of clinical tools that can help clinicians objectively assess oromotor and speech disorder and track progression of neurological diseases.

47 Chapter 4 – General Discussion Need for Automatic Objective Orofacial Assessments The goal of this thesis was to validate the use of 3D camera together with deep facial landmark tracking algorithms ( the FAN and the fine-tuned FAN) to extract orofacial features aimed at providing objective clinically interpretable measures / biomarkers to guide clinical decision making in diagnosis, prognosi s, and treatment management of neurological disorders. Neurological disorders are heterogenous in their clinical presentation , making them challenging to diagnose [140] . Furthermore, the lack of standards to follow in the clinical setting, and subjectivity of the existing methods used by ex perts can result in delays in obtaining diagnosis and delayed interventions and treatments . As a result, t he clinical management and patient experience is negatively impacted [141] . Studies have shown that subtle changes in speech and orofacial movements may be early signs of disease [8]. The use of markerless video -based technologies can provide clinicians with objective automatic analysis of facial movements and speech patterns that are non-invasive, cos t effective, and possibly sensitive to subtle changes that are not noticed by the clinicians [127] . In fact, video -based technologies have been used in o rofacial assessments to differentiate between groups of individuals with various neurological disease s and healthy control participant s [39], [40], [43], [106], [127] , as well as differentiat ing among various stages of neurological disease s [117], [127] .

Therefore, development of such technologies can help support clinicians in the assessment, diagnosis, prognosis, and treatment planning processes. AI-Vision based Automatic Assessment Machine learning and d eep learning techniques are the dominant methods used in automatically analyzing videos in the field of computer vision. New arc hitectures with higher accuracy are being actively developed in this fast-paced research community. While the current top performing algorithms may report high accuracy relative to manual human annotators on the dataset that they were trained on, they perf orm worse on datasets that they were not trained on [142] . The other limitation of such algorithms is their less -than-optimal performance in cases of occlusions such as glasses, facial hair, extreme poses, or diverse environmental conditions such as 48 lighting [45]. These events are extremely difficult to control, and they can compromise the data quality. In our work, we opt to perform data collection in a semi -structured environment, when a research assistant or clinician instruct s a patient/ participant to avoid occlusion and maintain optimized parameters for the duration of the recording session. In addition, m ost of these models are supervised methods that are trained on large amount of labelled data that may be inherently biased. In particular, landmark detection and tracking algorithms are mainly trained on the online available pictures of faces, which mostly co nsist of young subjects with light skin tone [143] .

A thorough analysis of available datasets have shown that these corpuses are indeed sex/ gender , race, and age imbalanced [142] . Therefore, as expected , off-the-shelf models are often biased toward s older participants , darker skin tone, and patient groups [86], [102], [144] . Training such algorithms on clinical data alone is not practical and unrealistic as the new state -of-the-art deep learning algorithms require large corpus of training data (in th e order of thousands to millions of samples). Since most clinical studies have relatively small sample size (~10 -150 participants per class) and strict restrictions on data sharing broadly among the research community, fine -tuning based on these samples re mains challenging . Nevertheless, technical solutions can be found to address some of the limitations and the inherent biases in these algorithms. Guarin et al. have shown that using examples from only 40 subjects in a controlled environment may be sufficient to improve the performance of the FAN model that was previously shown to be biased towards the older and/or clinical participants [145] . Fine-tuning is one of the effective methods of de-biasing existing state -of-the-art AI models [145] . Taa ti et al have also successfully applied this method to algorithms for facial tracking landmarks and have shown improved performance on tracking of facial features of older adults with dementia [102] . AI-based applications should be evaluated for their v alidity prior to their adoption .

This work used the well-established V3 framework [89] for the validation of digital biomarkers to evaluate the validity of the proposed video -based technology. As the technical verification of the used technology was previously established [34], [91] , this work aimed to further validate this technology both analytically , relative to a technical gold standard, and clinically , relative to an assessment by an expert clinician . Ultimately, robust clinically verified AI -based models need to be externally validated on a clinical cohort that adequately represents the target patient population from various collection sites in order to reduce the risks of overfitting and b iases [146] . 49 Analytical Validation In the first study of this thesis , the proposed technology was analytically validated relative to the gold standard highly precise and accurate technology – EMA. It was a pilot study that involved data collection from three healthy adults performing three standard assessment tasks (OPEN, SPREAD, BBP) at varying conditions (e.g. fast, slow, normal, and asymmetrical). The original intent was to expand the data set to more participants; however, the data collection was impacted by the COVID pandemic, and the study had to be stopped due to the inability to move to a remote data collection mode. This study is currently in progress – a data collection for 20 participants has been completed and will be analysed elsewhere. The kinematic features extracted in the pilot study , namely ranges of motion/ speeds etc.

associated with mouth opening and lips spreading during speech and non -speech tasks were compared between the two technologies using the zero -lag cross -correlation s and S pearman correlations. The zero -lag cross correlation measure showed strong agreement between the two methods when comparing the entire kinematic signal per repetition. However, Spearman correlations of the calculated averages and maximum values of kinemati c features showed mostly moderate correlations. Two conclusions were drawn from the results of this preliminary study. First, the 30 frames rate per second of the video camera can be a limiting factor since fast facial movement s may not be captured. Ouni e t al. recommended a minimum of 50 frames per second to ensure capturing all the critical information for fast movements such as speech [92]. Second, the jitteriness of the landmarks in the video resulted in “noisy ” distance signals and high variability of kinematic values extracted. In addition, this study demonstrated that there was high variability in the range of orofacial kinematics of orofacial movements performed by participants both within and in -between subjects. The high variance needs to be considered before making conclusions regarding the results of studies using such technologies. Finally, the strengths of the correlations were also dependant on the participants.

The highest correlations were obtained on the participant with the lightest skin tone, which is aligned with the findings of previous studies on the inherent biases of deep facial landmark detection algorithms [86], [102], [106], [144], [145] . This study also revealed some of the challenges and limitations of the proposed system. The RealSense video camera sometimes dropped video frames , which resulted in overall lower 50 video quality. A new recording software was developed in the lab to address this problem and inform the researchers if a specific recording need ed to be repeated, based on the number of dropped samples and overall low recording quality . The off -the-shelf FAN model used in this study showed high sensitivity to environmental factors such as variation in lighting as well as skin tone of participants . As such, fine-tuning procedures were established to improve FAN ’s performance and were used in the clinical validation study and elsewhere in our research . The main limitation of this study was the small sample size (n=3). Ongoing work focus es on full sample (n=17) evaluat ion of landmark detection and tracking algorithm on the data collected from EMA, 3D RealSense camera, and a laptop webcam [147] . The result of this study will be reported elsewhere and will include measures of inter -method agreement, test -retest consistency, and reliability of such technologies for performing automatic objective orofacial assessments. Clinical Validation In the second study, the proposed technology was clinically validated in the MND and Post-stroke groups.

Participants p erformed the same tasks as first study (OPEN, SPREAD, BBP) while being recorded in a controlled lab environment with a 3D camera. In this study, a fine -tuned model of the landmark detection and tracking algorithm on clinical data [85], [145] was used to automatically extract clinically relevant kinematic features from lips and jaw movements. A set of features in each clinical group showed moderate to good correlation with the perceptual clinical scores given by the expert clinician. The stati stically significant features within each task and clinical subgroup were representative of clinical characteristics anticipated by the group. The regression models built by the significant features were able to explain a good amount of variability within the data. High within and between subject variability was also observed in the extracted kinematic features in each of the categories of the severity scale , which highlights the challenges associated with heterogeneity of clinical characteristics and prese ntation s across clinical populations . A recent longitudinal study of post-stroke participants established a high internal consistency and reliability of such technologies with the ability to identify individual and task dependent differences over time [148] . 51 In this study, we only focused on three out of the five available clinical scores ; namely, ROM, speed, and symmetry. There were no objective kinematic measures defined/extracted for the two remaining scores of variability an d fatigue.

However, these measures are also important in characterizing neurological diseases. For example, individuals with ALS have reported fatigue as the most pre valent symptom occurring in 83% - 90% of patients compared with 20% in healthy control group [149] . Future studies should define objective and clinically interpretable measures for fatigue and variability sub-scores and include them for the full analysis . An example of a clinically interpretable objective measure to consider for f atigue is the rate of change in a given kinematic feature over consecutive repetitions of a task . It is expected that larger clinical scores of fatigue would translate to larger difference s betwee n kinematic features from early repetitions to final repetitions . Similarly, one can examine the standard deviation of a given kinematic feature over multiple repetitions as a potential marker of the variability sub -score. Finally, this study provided some insight s on the usefulness of different assessment tasks for quantif ying different aspects of orofacial impairments . For example , SPREAD is mainly used to assess symmetry of the horizontal lip motion, whi le OPEN assesses the vertical symmetry as well as the range of motion of the jaw and lips . Understanding the clinical importance and interpretation from each assessment task can help understand and validate such clinical tools as long as the automatic kinema tic features extracted remain clinically interpretable and relevant.

This study showed that predicting the severity o f clinical rating scales is challenging and may be limited by 1) the small sample size , and 2) the skewed distribution of the data across severity scale. Future studies should focus on gathering data from participants with more severe orofacial impairment in order to balance the dataset and have representative data in all the categories. Overall, this study provided evidence for validity of such technologies as objective assessment tools for orofacial movements. Conclusion The overall goal of this project was part of an effort to help the translation of novel video based technologies capa ble of providing objective kinematic measures into a useful clinical tool allowing for more accurate assessment of orofacial deficits within neurological dis order populations . Adopting the V3 framework for validation of digital biomarkers [89], the two studies 52 were performed on healthy, MND , and post -stroke participant groups to analytically and clinically validated such technologies for objective kinematic assessment of orofacial movements. measure provided by EMA. The combined results indicated that such technology can be used to automatically extract clinically meaningful and objective features that clinicians can incorporate into their workflow to make more informed and data -driven decisions . Limitations and Future Directions Upgrading the facial detection and tracking algorithm In present work, FAN [80] and fine -tuned FAN [85] models were used for facial landmark detection and tracking purposes.

FAN is a relatively old model (proposed in 2017 ) in the domain of deep facial t racking architectures , and lacks testing on wide spread face landmark datasets [61]. Current state -of-the-art models have reported higher performance and have been trained on larger and more diverse datasets than FAN. For example , PropagationNet, ADNet, and SubpixelHeatmap are more recent architectures that have achieved top performance on different datasets [61]. They are all based on the similar heatmap architecture as FAN and use Hourglass for their backbone with their own unique improvement ; PropagationNet introduced a new face boundary attention module, ADNet separates normal and tangent errors , while SubpixelHeatmap focuses on reduc ing heatmap quantization error [61]. The higher performance of these algorithms on datasets used for training and testing FAN as well as more challenging datasets [61] suggest their superior performance and higher facial landmark detection and tracking on the clinical data collected for these studies. Using the methods in [85] and clinical orofacial datasets such as [73], these models can be further fine -tuned to more accurately track the facial features in clinical populations. Increasing the performance of landmark detection and tracki ng algorithms will directly translate to extracting more accurate kinematic features and thus development of more reliable and accurate tool for assessing orofacial movements.

Using all the spatial and temporal data from facial landmarks The current studies used the values of kinematic features averaged across multiple repetitions which provided a single discrete value per kinematic feature , task, and participant . This results in loss of potentially useful information within each repetition as well as more broadly looking at the values of features across multiple repetitions . For example, useful information 53 regarding fatigue and variability clinical scores can be obtained by looking at the changes of the kinematic measures acr oss multiple repetitions in tasks such as sentence repetition. Future work may explore using time series data of kinematic features rather than single values to potentially capture more clinically relevant features. For example, in a study using an LSTM architecture on the time series of facial landmarks data obtained from participants expressing specific emotions in front of camera was used to distinguish between individuals with Parkinson disease and healthy controls [100] . In this study LSTM showed promising result s in analyzing sequential video data to guide clinicians with diagnosis of neurological diseases. The main limitation of this study was small sample size [100] . In the future, as more clinical data becomes available, such architecture s can be used to derive clinically meaningful features that are sensitive to temporal changes within multiple repetitions. Multimodal data This work only considered 3D video data (colour and depth).

Recent studies have shown that multimodal data (both video and audio) can further improve the predictive power of such algorithms [117], [150] –[153] . For example, Neumann et al. demonstrated that combining acoustic signals obtained from the audio signal (i.e. features obtained from acoustic analysis) together with facial kinematic features results in increase predictive power of these such algorithms [117] . Using a set of features extracted from video a s well as audio signals, the trained algorithms showed promising results in differentiat ing between healthy controls, pre -symptomatic ALS, and bulbar ALS , thus, demonstrating the utility of incorporating various speech and facial metrics to assist early diagnosis and monitoring of ALS [117] . Similarl y, the future work of this research should consider extrac ting clinically valid speech features from audio recordings of the speech tasks to potentially increase the predictive power of the regression models . Collecting Longitudinal Data These technologies have a great potential for diagnosis and prognosis of neurological disorders as larger volumes of longitudinal data, accounting for objective inter and intra subject variability, is being gathered [154] . Current clinical practice suffe rs from one-shot assessments, where patients see clinicians once every few months or even years [149] . The performance of the patient may not provide a true picture of their performance due to the day -to-day variability of the 54 disease characteristics [154].

This is of particular importance in neurological disorders in which the disease representation itself is heterogenous and patient’s clinical characteristics may vary from day -to-day [139], [154] . Therefore, collecting repeated assessments reduce in tra-participant’s variability while increasing the power of the study [154] . In addition, longitudinal study design can be more efficient, cost effective, and result in more robust model selection process [155] . Previously longitudinal orofacial and speech home -based assessments in neurological disorders resulted in characteristics of disease progression and/or response to treatment interventions [96].
